topic: Trail — site scoring formula (exact mechanics)
detail: Trail scores every task: 10 points on-time, 5 points late, 0 incomplete. Points are averaged across all tasks to give a score out of 10. Colour bands: green ≥9.6, orange 7.6–9.5, red ≤7.5, grey = site still 'Setting Up'. The headline average covers the last 30 consecutive days and is compared against the previous 30. Scores are locked at end of business day — tasks not synced by then count as missed and Trail explicitly refuses retroactive recalculation ('We do not change scores for compliance reasons'). Only Manager/Admin users see scores, and managers only for their assigned sites.
why_it_matters: This is the single most copyable mechanic found. It is dead simple (3-value scoring), needs no weighting config, and produces a defensible number. The 'no retroactive recalculation' stance is a deliberate integrity decision, not a limitation — it stops managers gaming the score after the fact.
decision_needed: Do we adopt Trail's 10/5/0 on-time/late/incomplete model as-is, and do we lock scores at end of day with no retro edits?

topic: Trail — five distinct schedule types, not just 'recurring'
detail: Trail separates schedules into five types: (1) Repeat — daily/weekly/monthly/multi-month/yearly, stackable so a task can appear several times a day; (2) One-Off — specific appear/due dates, used for launches and announcements; (3) Ad Hoc — no schedule, staff pull it from the Complete Tasks page on demand; (4) Action — corrective-action templates triggered by another task's response; (5) Automatic — fired by external integrations (Zapier). Tasks have start AND end date/times so they cannot be completed prematurely, and work is bucketed into 'timeslots' derived from the site's configured business hours.
why_it_matters: The timeslot concept is the key insight: hospitality doesn't run on clock times, it runs on 'open', 'changeover', 'close'. Modelling tasks against trading-day timeslots rather than absolute times is what makes these apps fit a pub. The start-time gate (can't tick the closing checks at 3pm) is an anti-gaming control we would be foolish to omit.
decision_needed: Do we model task windows against configurable trading-day timeslots (open/mid/close) rather than absolute clock times?

topic: Trail — 'Record Logs' are the must-input-a-value primitive
detail: Trail splits data capture into Table format (multiple record rows in one task, with a settable minimum expected number of records and the option to stop teams adding/removing rows — recommended for fridge temperature rounds) and Form format (one record per task — for audits, accident reports). Six field types: Text, Number, Date & Time, Options, File/Photo, Section Title. Number fields take prefixes (£) and suffixes (°C).
why_it_matters: The Table-vs-Form split is the industry answer to 'log five fridges' vs 'fill in one report'. The 'minimum expected number of records' + locked rows is what stops staff logging three fridges out of five and calling it done.
decision_needed: Do we need both a repeating-rows table type and a single-form type at launch, or is table-only enough for v1?

topic: Trail — out-of-range values auto-raise exceptions
detail: Number fields support out-of-range warnings. Acceptable ranges can be driven conditionally by an Options field — e.g. selecting 'fridge' vs 'freezer' applies a different acceptable temperature band to the same numeric field. Values outside the band 'automatically count towards an exception', and a Corrective Action can be attached so the team must raise a follow-up action then and there.
why_it_matters: This is the de-facto standard pattern and the whole point of digital temperature logs: capture → validate against a band → auto-exception → forced corrective action, in one flow. A temperature log that just stores a number is a paper log in a database. The option-driven range is what avoids building a separate task per appliance.
decision_needed: Should acceptable ranges live on the appliance/asset record rather than the task template, so ranges are maintained in one place?

topic: Jolt — Bluetooth probes produce 'un-editable' records
detail: Jolt uses Bluetooth-enabled probes that sync directly into the app, creating instant records the company explicitly markets as 'un-editable'. It automates the timing of hot-holding and cooling readings and prompts staff when the next reading is due. Ranges tracked include hot-hold ≥135°F/57°C, fridge ≤41°F/5°C, freezer 0°F/-18°C, cooling 135→70°F within 2h then 70→41°F within 4h, reheat ≥165°F.
why_it_matters: The selling point is not convenience, it is the removal of human hands from the number — i.e. anti-falsification. Any manual-entry temperature system is, to an auditor, a system where staff can type '4' from the car park. If we stay manual-entry we should be explicit about that trade-off and compensate elsewhere (timestamps, geofence, photo).
decision_needed: Do we accept manual keyed temperatures for v1, and if so what compensating control (photo of the probe display? timestamp proximity?) do we use?

topic: Jolt — cooling/time-bound processes with escalating visual countdown
detail: For multi-stage checks (like cooling), Jolt runs a timed process with a colour-changing alert: green = process started, amber = check due in 15 min, red = due in 10 min, flashing red = due in 5 min, plus push notifications to team devices.
why_it_matters: Reveals that some compliance tasks are not 'due at a time' but 'due N minutes after a triggering event', with a live countdown. That's a fundamentally different scheduling model from cron-style recurrence and is easy to overlook when designing the data model.
decision_needed: Do we need event-triggered relative-time tasks (start a cooling process → next check due +2h), or is that out of scope for a pub?

topic: Jolt — PIN login on shared devices is the standard attribution mechanism
detail: Jolt has an explicit 'Shared Device Mode': multiple users log in and out via QR code or PIN. Employees tap Login, select their name, enter their PIN. Unique PINs let staff skip the name-selection step for faster login. Jolt attributes completion with photo-proof, time stamps and name stamps.
why_it_matters: This is the de-facto industry standard and we would be foolish to omit it. The whole category assumes one wall-mounted tablet behind the bar, not personal phones. Individual accounts with email/password logins would collapse in a pub — attribution has to be a 4-digit PIN against a staff record, resolving to a name stamp on the record. Note the pattern: the device is authenticated persistently; the *person* is authenticated per-action.
decision_needed: Do we implement a per-action PIN attribution model on a persistently-signed-in shared device, rather than per-user sessions?

topic: Jolt — the accountability triad: photo-proof + time stamp + name stamp
detail: Jolt markets 'complete team accountability' as exactly three things bound to every completion: a photo, a timestamp, and a name stamp. It also automates task assignment, corrective actions, and notifications for overdue/incomplete tasks.
why_it_matters: Every vendor in this category converges on this same triad. It is the minimum viable evidence bundle. If our completion record does not carry all three, it is weaker than every competitor and weaker than what an EHO now expects to see.

topic: Trail — EHO/Primary Authority approval is a commercial moat, not a feature
detail: Trail holds an assured Primary Authority partnership and states it is approved by EHOs for record keeping, positioning itself as proof of HACCP compliance in an inspection. It keeps a full digital audit log, comment threads and image uploads as proof for management and auditors.
why_it_matters: For a UK pub this is the actual purchase reason. A Primary Authority partnership means an EHO has pre-blessed the record format. We cannot replicate that credential, but we can and should match the record format so our exports stand up in an inspection — the historical record, immutable and time-stamped, is the product.
decision_needed: Is EHO-defensible record-keeping an explicit requirement for us, or are we building an internal ops tool that does not claim compliance?

topic: Crunchtime Ops Execution (Zenput) — issue → auto-assigned corrective task → escalation to closure
detail: Zenput schedules recurring and ad hoc work auto-assigned to employees. When an issue arises, staff are instantly assigned follow-up tasks and corrective actions, and managers receive alerts and status updates. Reports carry photos, notes and geo-tags and escalate instantly to supervisors. The platform tracks 'corrective action to closure' with escalation.
why_it_matters: 'To closure' is the load-bearing phrase. Flagging a problem is trivial; the industry standard is that a failure opens a tracked item with an owner and a deadline that cannot be silently dropped. Geo-tagging on reports is also notable — it proves the check happened at the site.
decision_needed: Do exceptions become first-class tracked records with an owner and a due date, or just flags on a completed task?

topic: Crunchtime/Zenput — cross-store benchmarking as the manager's primary view
detail: Data from tasks, audits and incidents feeds analytics dashboards where leaders compare performance across stores, track completion rates and identify underperforming units. Reported outcomes: 20% improvement in audit scores, 24% reduction in compliance issues, 5 hrs/week saved for store employees and 8 hrs/week for field managers. Used across 60,000+ locations.
why_it_matters: Confirms the buyer is the multi-site manager, and the killer view is a ranked comparison of sites. For a single-site operator like The Anchor the equivalent is ranking *people* or *shifts/day-parts*, not sites — the same mechanic pointed at a different axis.
decision_needed: For a single site, do we rank by staff member, by day-part, or by task area?

topic: SafetyCulture/iAuditor — weighted scoring via response sets
detail: Scoring works by assigning numeric values to multiple-choice answers within a response set — e.g. great=3, satisfactory=2, passable=1, negative=0 — which roll into an inspection scorecard. One response in the set (e.g. 'Non-Compliant') is designated the *failed response*; selecting it flags the item as failed and can trigger an Action.
why_it_matters: This is the more sophisticated alternative to Trail's flat 10/5/0. It scores *quality of answer*, not just *did you do it*. Trail scores compliance-with-schedule; SafetyCulture scores compliance-with-standard. They are genuinely different products and we should pick deliberately — most operators eventually want both.
decision_needed: Are we scoring 'did the task get done on time' (Trail model) or 'how well did the site perform' (SafetyCulture model)? These need different data models.

topic: SafetyCulture — Actions can be assigned to people without an account
detail: An Action raised from a flagged inspection item takes a due date, a priority level, and an assignee — and can be assigned to someone who does not have a SafetyCulture account.
why_it_matters: Removes the classic dead-end where a failed check needs the plumber/landlord/contractor to fix it but they aren't a licensed user, so the action dies in someone's head. Worth copying for anything involving external contractors.

topic: SafetyCulture — offline-first with configurable sync interval
detail: Inspections can be stored locally on the device so they can be edited and reports generated with no connection. Syncing is automatic when online (Wi-Fi or cellular per settings) and the sync interval is user-configurable — e.g. every 1 minute, 30 minutes or 1 hour.
why_it_matters: Offline is a hard requirement in this category — cellars, walk-in fridges and kitchens have no signal, and that is precisely where temperature checks happen. Note this collides directly with Trail's 'unsynced by end of day = missed' rule: an offline-first design must decide whether the completion timestamp is the capture time or the sync time. Capture time is the only honest answer.
decision_needed: Do we commit to offline capture in v1, and do we timestamp on capture (correct, harder) or on sync (easy, wrong)?

topic: Squadle — ZeroTouch probe, 83% faster temperature capture
detail: Squadle (now part of Crunchtime) began as a tablet replacing paper logs and added patented ZeroTouch technology that transfers a reading from the thermometer into the digital checklist automatically, claiming food/equipment temperatures taken 83% faster than a manual thermometer. Personalised dashboards with text and email alerts give a real-time pulse on activity and compliance per store.
why_it_matters: Quantifies why probe integration wins: it is a speed argument as much as an integrity one. Staff defeat slow systems. Any manual temperature flow we build must be brutally fast — the number of taps to log five fridges is the metric that decides whether the product gets used or quietly abandoned.

topic: Xenia — conditional logic driving corrective actions and notifications
detail: Xenia's template builder embeds conditional logic in each step: a form response can trigger a corrective action on a failed inspection step, or notify management on out-of-range meter readings. Answering 'No' to a safety question reveals follow-up questions automatically. Audits support conditional visibility plus weighted-and-nullify scoring. Failed steps can spawn work orders, with corrective data stored inside the checklist submission for auditing.
why_it_matters: 'Nullify' scoring is subtle and important — an N/A question must be removed from the denominator, not scored zero, or sites get punished for equipment they don't have. Any scoring model needs an explicit N/A path. Also note corrective data is stored *inside* the submission, keeping the evidence bundle together.
decision_needed: Does our scoring support N/A that nullifies (drops from denominator) rather than scoring zero?

topic: Xenia — QR, no-login work order requests
detail: Xenia supports 'QR no-login work orders' — a QR sticker on equipment lets anyone report a fault (the broken fryer at 11pm) and have it logged and routed without installing an app or logging in.
why_it_matters: Clever friction removal for the report-a-problem path specifically. The insight: the person who *finds* a problem is often not a system user, and forcing a login means the problem never gets reported. Worth considering a QR-per-appliance pattern.

topic: 7shifts — tasks attached to shifts, not to dates
detail: 7shifts attaches recurring tasks to shifts created in the weekly schedule, divided by location, department or role. Once scheduled, employees see the tasks for their shift and are reminded during it. Managers see who completed each task and when. Completion types include photos, values and temperature readouts.
why_it_matters: A genuinely different scheduling philosophy from Trail's: tasks hang off the rota rather than the calendar. Because we already own the rota (rota_shifts), this is the cheapest possible path to both scheduling AND attribution — if you're clocked into the shift, the tasks are yours and completion attributes to you automatically. This is likely our unfair advantage.
decision_needed: Do we bind task assignment to rota shifts/roles (leveraging our existing rota) rather than building a parallel assignment system?

topic: Checkit — sensors displace the manual check entirely
detail: Checkit pairs a guided digital assistant on a handheld ('Memo') that displays scheduled tasks and auto-date-stamps actions, with handheld probes and wireless IoT sensors monitoring temperature, humidity or door status 24/7, alerting immediately when conditions stray beyond limits. It explicitly handles both scheduled and unscheduled tasks.
why_it_matters: The strategic direction of the whole category: the best manual check is no manual check. Continuous sensor monitoring makes the 'log the fridge twice a day' task obsolete and produces better evidence. Trail confirms the same direction ('connects to fridges and sensors to automate repetitive checks'). Worth designing the data model so a reading's source can be human OR sensor from day one, even if we never ship sensors.
decision_needed: Should the temperature reading model be source-agnostic (manual | probe | sensor) from the start to avoid a painful migration later?

topic: FoodDocs — 'smart prefill' of monitoring logs is a compliance anti-pattern
detail: FoodDocs markets eliminating repetitive tasks via 'pre-filled monitoring checks based on previously inputted data (you simply confirm the logs or change them)', AI-generated pre-set monitoring tasks from the business profile, and a flexible schedule letting sites pause or skip monitoring during holidays or emergencies.
why_it_matters: Flagging this as a deliberate DO-NOT-COPY. Pre-filling yesterday's fridge temperature and asking staff to confirm is functionally 'pencil-whipping' with a nicer UI — it trains staff to tap Confirm without looking, and it is exactly the falsification that Jolt's un-editable probe records and Squadle's ZeroTouch exist to prevent. Two vendors in this same list build their entire value proposition on preventing this. The pause/skip schedule is legitimate and useful though — pubs close.
decision_needed: Confirm we will never pre-fill a measured value — but do we want a legitimate pause/skip for closure days?

topic: Trail — access control on scores and task editing
detail: Only admin-level users can edit task schedules via the Task Editor's Schedule tab. Site scores are Manager/Admin only, scoped to assigned sites, and gated behind the Standard Plan. Trail also ships Custom Roles for creating, editing and assigning tasks to sites.
why_it_matters: Confirms the permission split we'd need: staff complete, managers verify and see scores, admins define templates and schedules. Critically, the person doing the task must not be able to edit the task or its schedule — that separation is the integrity control. Maps cleanly onto our existing RBAC (staff/manager/super_admin).

topic: Trail — reporting is export-oriented, built for the inspection moment
detail: Trail provides live dashboards and reports for remote visibility, plus Task Reports to review site history and export/download data. Jolt likewise exports all temperature records to Excel or Google Sheets for audit compliance.
why_it_matters: The reporting requirement is narrower than it first appears. Nobody wants BI — they want to hand an EHO a clean, complete, time-stamped history for a date range, on the spot. A well-built export against a date range covers most of the real need; dashboards are for the owner, not the inspector.
decision_needed: Is the v1 reporting deliverable simply a date-range export of completions with evidence, rather than dashboards?

topic: Category convergence — the non-negotiable feature floor
detail: Across Trail, Jolt, Zenput, SafetyCulture, Xenia, Squadle, Checkit, MeazureUp and 7shifts, the same set recurs without exception: recurring + ad-hoc scheduling; time-windowed tasks; numeric entry with acceptable ranges; auto-exception on out-of-range; corrective action tracked to closure; photo evidence; name + time stamp on every completion; shared-device PIN attribution; offline capture; and a completion-rate score compared across sites/periods.
why_it_matters: This is the answer to 'what would we be foolish to omit'. Anything missing from this list reads as an unfinished product to anyone who has used a competitor. Conversely, everything beyond this list (gamification, AI template generation, IoT) is differentiation, not table stakes.
decision_needed: Do we agree this list is the v1 scope floor, with sensors/AI explicitly deferred?

topic: Yoobic — gamification with points, badges and leaderboards
detail: Yoobic layers mobile-first training with gamification: quizzes, gamified head-to-head 'battles', points and badges, and a leaderboard showing top performers. It is an employee experience platform for frontline teams spanning comms, task management and training — but is only sold to businesses with 100+ locations or 1000+ users.
why_it_matters: Gamification is real but is positioned as an engagement/training layer for large estates, not core ops — and no other vendor in this list leads with it. For a single pub, a leaderboard of staff task scores is socially risky (it can read as public shaming) and Trail's choice to hide scores from staff entirely is a deliberate counterpoint worth respecting.
decision_needed: Do staff see their own completion score, or is scoring manager-only as in Trail?

topic: MeazureUp — the two-app split: daily checks vs periodic field audits
detail: MeazureUp deliberately ships two separate products: DailyChex (a daily checklist app for the team on-site) and AuditApp (field inspections done by a visiting auditor/area manager), across 6000+ locations for brands like MOD Pizza and Spur. It emphasises setting benchmarks, trend analysis and historical reports.
why_it_matters: The clearest statement in the research that the daily-checklist job and the manager-verification job are different products with different users, cadences and scoring. This maps directly to the 'manager verification / spot-check' requirement: a spot-check is not a task on the same list, it is a separate audit *of* the list, done by a different person, scored differently.
decision_needed: Do we model manager spot-checks as a distinct audit entity that references and scores completed tasks, rather than as just another task type?

topic: SFBB is the default legal route for a pub kitchen
detail: Regulation (EC) 852/2004 requires a documented food safety management system based on HACCP principles. For small caterers the FSA's Safer Food Better Business (SFBB) pack is the recognised way to meet this. It covers six areas: cross-contamination, cleaning, chilling, cooking, management, and using the diary. The 'Safe Methods' sheets are the standing procedures; the Diary is the daily evidence they were followed.
why_it_matters: A digital checklist system for a pub is really a digital SFBB. If it doesn't map onto the four Cs plus management plus diary, an EHO won't recognise it as the FSMS and the pub is technically operating without one.
decision_needed: Do we model our checklist schema explicitly on the SFBB structure (safe methods + daily diary + 4-weekly review), or invent our own taxonomy?

topic: Daily diary completion is the minimum bar
detail: FSA guidance: the diary must be completed for all food operations, with daily records, as a minimum every day the business trades. The diary also contains a mandatory four-weekly review to confirm the system is still current and to spot recurring problems.
why_it_matters: The system needs a per-trading-day completion concept (not just ad-hoc checks) and a separate recurring 4-weekly manager review artefact. Most checklist apps miss the 4-weekly review entirely.
decision_needed: Do we build the 4-weekly review as a first-class scheduled record type with its own sign-off?

topic: Digital records are explicitly accepted — FSA says so in writing
detail: FSA/GOV.UK SFBB guidance explicitly offers two options: download the diary pages and 'store them electronically', or print them. Records may be kept on paper or electronically. Commercial digital SFBB products (SFBB+, SFBB Pro — the latter approved via a primary authority partnership with Dorset Council) are in active use and recognised by enforcement bodies.
why_it_matters: There is no legal blocker to going fully paperless. This kills the most common objection from a cautious licensee or EHO. It also means the deliverable must be exportable/viewable on demand during an inspection.
decision_needed: Should we pursue a Primary Authority partnership for formal EHO recognition, or rely on the generic FSA acceptance of electronic records?

topic: The inspector must be able to see the records on the day, on site
detail: FSA guidance says store completed diary pages 'safely until your next visit from a local authority food safety officer' — officers 'may want to look at your diary pages'. Inspections are unannounced.
why_it_matters: Availability is a hard requirement. A system that is offline, locked behind one manager's login, or needs head-office to export it will fail an inspection even if the data is perfect. Needs an on-site read-only 'inspector view' and offline resilience.
decision_needed: Do we build a dedicated inspector/read-only mode and an instant PDF export of any date range?

topic: Retention period is not fixed in law — and vendor claims conflict badly
detail: Reg (EC) 852/2004 requires records 'commensurate with the nature and size of the business' retained for 'an appropriate period'; the FSA says it is for the business to decide, based on shelf life. FSA has indicated a minimum of around 3 months; most EH departments advise 12 months+. Traceability files for meat/shellfish/long-shelf-life goods: up to 5 years, or shelf life plus one year. One vendor blog claims a flat '6 years' obligation — this appears unsupported by the regulation and should not be relied on.
why_it_matters: There is no single number to hard-code. Retention must be configurable, with a safe default. Deleting records at 3 months would be defensible in law but disastrous in an EHO conversation or a prosecution 18 months later.
decision_needed: Set default retention to 24 months (safely beyond any EHO expectation and typical inspection cycles) and make it configurable — agreed?

topic: Chilled food: 8°C is the legal maximum, not the target
detail: Food Safety and Hygiene (England) Regulations 2013, Schedule 4: it is an offence to keep food that supports pathogen growth above 8°C. Industry best practice is 5°C or below, precisely to leave headroom before the legal limit is breached.
why_it_matters: A checklist should treat 5°C as the 'green' target and 8°C as the hard fail, with an amber band between. Recording a fridge at 7.5°C as a plain 'pass' hides a system drifting toward an offence.
decision_needed: Do we implement three-band thresholds (green/amber/red) rather than binary pass/fail on temperature checks?

topic: Hot holding is 63°C, with a two-hour tolerance
detail: Schedule 4: cooked/reheated food for service must be held at or above 63°C. A defence exists where food is held below 63°C for less than two hours and has not previously been held that way. Chilled display has a parallel four-hour rule (food out of chill for under four hours, not previously abused).
why_it_matters: These are time-bounded defences, not standing permissions. A digital system can actually enforce them (start a timer at removal from chill/hot-hold) — something paper physically cannot do. Genuine differentiator.
decision_needed: Do we build countdown timers for the 4-hour chilled display and 2-hour hot-hold defences?

topic: The 8°C rule has documented defences that need capturing
detail: Schedule 4 defences include: manufacturer's recommendation of a higher temperature backed by well-founded scientific assessment and communicated to the operator; brief unavoidable exceedance during defrosting, processing practicalities, or temporary equipment breakdown, where consistent with food safety.
why_it_matters: A red temperature reading is not automatically an offence. If the system only records 'FAIL' with no place to log the defence (breakdown, defrost cycle, manufacturer spec), it manufactures evidence against the pub.
decision_needed: Should a failed reading force a structured corrective-action entry with a defence/reason category?

topic: Cellar temperature is quality, not food safety law — different rules apply
detail: Cask ale cellars run at 11–13°C; that is the trade/brewer standard (Beer Genius/Molson Coors best practice), driven by beer condition — too cold causes chill haze, too warm causes rapid spoilage. It is NOT the 8°C food safety limit. Beer lines should be cleaned every 7 days, and cask lines fully cleaned at every cask change.
why_it_matters: Critical distinction: a cellar at 12°C is correct, not a breach. If the system applies the 8°C food rule to a cellar probe it will generate constant false failures and staff will learn to ignore alerts. Beer line cleaning is a separate 7-day recurring task.
decision_needed: Do we type checkpoints by purpose (food-safety vs quality) so each carries its own thresholds and legal weight?

topic: FHRS scoring: three elements, and the lowest one caps your rating
detail: Three scored elements: hygiene compliance, structural compliance, and confidence in management (scored 0/5/10/20/30 — lower is better). Totals map to ratings: 0–15 = 5, 20 = 4, 25–30 = 3, 35–40 = 2, 45–50 = 1, over 50 = 0. Crucially there is a per-element cap: no single element may exceed 5 points for a 5 rating, 10 for a 4, etc. A weak element cannot be offset by strong ones.
why_it_matters: This is the commercial hook. Confidence in management is the element records most directly drive, and because of the cap, a bad management score alone drops the rating regardless of how clean the kitchen is. A 30 on management makes a 5 arithmetically impossible.
decision_needed: Do we surface a live 'confidence in management' readiness indicator to the licensee as the headline value proposition?

topic: Confidence in management is literally scored on the records you keep
detail: EHOs score confidence in management on evidence that standards are sustained between visits, because an inspection is only a snapshot. Cited evidence: a documented food safety management system, staff training records, and logged storage temperature checks. Officers also interview staff to test understanding. Example given: a premises scores 20 (bad) because the FSMS is not documented and they don't understand how it should work; 5 (good) where hygiene, structural and management practices are all sound.
why_it_matters: Complete digital records are the single highest-leverage input to the FHRS rating. This directly connects the checklist product to a number displayed on the pub's front door and on Just Eat/Deliveroo listings.
decision_needed: Should the system also hold staff training records, given they are explicitly part of the same score?

topic: Missing records are what an EHO looks for first — and they collapse the due diligence defence
detail: Section 21 Food Safety Act 1990 gives a defence if the business proves it took all reasonable precautions and exercised all due diligence. Practitioner guidance: gaps in temperature records are among the first things an inspector flags; a two-week gap in fridge logs means 'the defence starts to crumble'. Corrective actions and retraining must also be recorded.
why_it_matters: Gaps are the risk, not imperfect readings. The system's job is to make gaps visible and rare — and to record corrective actions, which are as legally important as the check itself and are usually the thing paper systems omit.
decision_needed: Do we build a gap/exception dashboard for the licensee, so missed checks are chased same-day rather than discovered by an EHO?

topic: Back-filled records are detected fast and actively harm the pub
detail: Practitioner guidance: 'EHOs spot back-filled records in the first 15 minutes on site.' HACCP audit guidance lists classic tells: identical handwriting/one person completing every record with no variation, no gaps in an implausibly perfect run, and logged readings that don't match actual equipment readings.
why_it_matters: A digital system that lets a manager retro-complete a week of checks at 5pm Friday actively creates evidence of falsification. Timestamps make the fraud provable in a way paper never did — this is a genuine liability if designed carelessly.
decision_needed: Do we prohibit silent back-dating entirely — allow late entries but stamp them immutably as 'completed late' with the true server time and a reason?

topic: Falsifying records is treated far more seriously than failing a check
detail: In 2015 a chef and manager at an Essex hotel were jailed for perverting the course of justice by falsifying temperature and cooling records. Sentencing guidance reserves the highest penalties for deliberate breaches, ignored warnings, obstruction, and falsified records. Food safety offences carry unlimited fines and up to 2 years' imprisonment. Directors, owners and managers can be personally prosecuted where an offence occurred with their consent, connivance or neglect.
why_it_matters: A late or missing check is a compliance failing. A faked one is potentially a custodial criminal offence with personal liability for the licensee. The product must make honest late entry the path of least resistance and make fabrication impossible.
decision_needed: Do we make the audit trail immutable and non-editable (append-only corrections, never overwrites) as a core design principle?

topic: A late record is recoverable; a fabricated one is not
detail: Synthesising the above: a genuine late entry with an honest timestamp and a recorded reason still evidences a functioning system and supports due diligence. A back-filled entry, once detected, destroys the credibility of every other record the business holds and pushes the case toward deliberate-breach sentencing and personal liability.
why_it_matters: This is the core design philosophy for the whole product. The system should never punish honesty in a way that tempts staff to lie. An entry marked 'completed 3 hours late — probe was being recalibrated' is an asset; a clean fake is a criminal exhibit.
decision_needed: Do we accept the principle that the system always accepts a late entry, never blocks it, but always marks it truthfully?

topic: Traceability and allergen records are a separate legal duty
detail: It is a legal requirement to record what food products were bought, from whom, quantity and date, plus allergen information. Traceability and waste transfer records are also legally required. Traceability files for meat, shellfish, or long-shelf-life products may need retaining up to 5 years, or shelf life plus one year.
why_it_matters: This sits outside the daily-checks loop but an EHO will ask for it. It carries a materially longer retention period than daily diary records — so retention cannot be a single global setting.
decision_needed: Do we scope traceability/allergen records into v1, or explicitly park them and set per-record-type retention now so the schema supports them later?

topic: Licensing daily duties: no statutory daily check, but a refusals register is the practical norm
detail: There are six mandatory conditions under the Licensing Act 2003 (irresponsible promotions, potable water, age verification policy, small measures, plus DPS requirements). Notably, daily physical checks are NOT a statutory mandatory condition — but licensing authorities and police routinely impose them as premises-specific conditions. An age verification policy is mandatory; Challenge 25 is the standard implementation, with posters required and refusals recorded 'whether on paper or in digital form'. The DPS must ensure alcohol sales follow the age verification policy.
why_it_matters: Licensing conditions are per-premises, not universal — so the licensing side of the checklist must be configurable to the actual licence, not hard-coded. The refusals register is the one licensing record with explicit digital acceptance and is a natural fit.
decision_needed: Do we build a configurable licensing-conditions module driven by the individual premises licence, with a digital refusals register as the anchor feature?

topic: H&S records a pub must keep, separate from food safety
detail: Fire: recorded checks of alarm systems, emergency lighting, extinguishers and fire doors (weekly alarm test is standard practice). RIDDOR 2013: responsible persons must report AND keep records of reportable workplace accidents/injuries; accident investigations should retain initial reports, follow-ups, photos and statements. Legionella (HSE ACOP L8): bacteria grow between 20°C and 45°C, especially where water stagnates; temperature monitoring records per outlet marked pass/fail, hot and cold sections separated with location detail. Legionella risk is elevated in pubs by little-used guest room or function room outlets.
why_it_matters: A pub's real daily/weekly checklist spans three regulators (EHO for food, licensing authority, HSE for H&S) — not one. Legionella outlet monitoring in particular has its own temperature logic (avoid 20–45°C) that is the inverse of food chilling logic, and is commonly neglected in pubs.
decision_needed: Do we scope H&S (fire, RIDDOR, legionella) into the same system as food and licensing, or ship food-only first?

topic: Three different temperature regimes, three different rulebooks
detail: Summary of the conflicting ranges a single pub must manage simultaneously: chilled food ≤8°C legal / ≤5°C best practice; freezer typically -18°C; hot holding ≥63°C; cooking core commonly 75°C; cask cellar 11–13°C (quality standard, not law); legionella danger zone 20–45°C (must AVOID, so hot water must be hot and cold water cold).
why_it_matters: Any generic 'temperature check' field is wrong. Each checkpoint needs its own thresholds, its own direction of failure, its own legal basis, and its own consequence. Getting this wrong produces alert fatigue that undermines the entire system.
decision_needed: Do we model checkpoints as typed templates with per-type thresholds, units, legal basis and failure direction, rather than a generic numeric field?

topic: RRULE is a calendar-anchored generator, not a task scheduler
detail: RFC 5545's RRULE expands from a fixed DTSTART anchor using FREQ/INTERVAL/BYxxx parts. Every occurrence is a pure function of (DTSTART, RRULE, RDATE, EXDATE) — nothing in the spec can reference the state of a previous instance, including whether it was done or when. VTODO has COMPLETED and PERCENT-COMPLETE properties, but RRULE expansion ignores them entirely. RFC 5545 also forbids COUNT and UNTIL in the same rule, and mandates that occurrences not matching DTSTART's value type are ignored.
why_it_matters: This is the single hard boundary of the standard: if you adopt RRULE as your recurrence model, you have adopted calendar-based scheduling and cannot express 'due 14 days after it was last done' without inventing a non-standard extension. Most products that appear to do both actually run two separate engines and only export the RRULE half to iCal.
decision_needed: Do we store recurrence as an RRULE string (interoperable, exports to iCal/Google Calendar) or as our own structured columns (expressive enough for completion-anchored rules, but no calendar export)?

topic: The 'every-N' vs 'after-N' split is the cleanest available primitive
detail: Home Assistant's Chore Helper models exactly two scheduling modes: every-n ("scheduled based on start date") and after-n ("scheduled based on completion date"). Grocy makes the same cut with period types manually / dynamic-regular / daily / weekly / monthly / yearly, where dynamic-regular means "after you have performed the task it is scheduled again a set number of days after you last performed it". MaintainX calls the same distinction fixed interval vs floating interval.
why_it_matters: Four separate products in different domains converged on the same binary. That is strong evidence it is the right primitive rather than a quirk. It also means the schema needs one discriminator column (anchor: 'schedule' | 'completion') rather than a proliferation of recurrence types.
decision_needed: Is the anchor mode a per-task setting, or fixed per task category (e.g. compliance checks always calendar-anchored, deep cleans always completion-anchored)?

topic: Floating/completion-anchored schedules drift, and the drift is the point — but it cuts both ways
detail: With floating intervals, a 30-day task completed 5 days late schedules the next one 30 days from the completion, not from the original due date. MaintainX notes that the recalculation happens on completion and applies equally to early completion — finish early and the next due date pulls forward with it, so the schedule silently accelerates.
why_it_matters: Drift is desirable for wear-based work (a deep clean done late genuinely doesn't need doing again 25 days later) and dangerous for compliance work (a monthly fire-alarm test that drifts 5 days each cycle falls out of a regulatory month within a year). Early completion also lets keen staff quietly increase the workload for everyone.
decision_needed: Should completion-anchored tasks clamp — never schedule the next instance earlier than the original due date, so early completion doesn't accelerate the cadence?

topic: Fixed intervals pile up work orders; floating intervals guarantee at most one
detail: MaintainX's docs are explicit: fixed intervals can leave multiple work orders open simultaneously because the schedule keeps firing regardless of completion, whereas floating intervals "attempt to keep only one work order open at a time" because the next target isn't computed until the current one closes.
why_it_matters: This is the real operational trade-off behind the anchor choice, and it's usually more visible to staff than the drift question. A neglected calendar-anchored daily task produces 30 angry red rows in a month; the same task floating produces exactly one. The first is an honest audit trail, the second is a usable to-do list.
decision_needed: For calendar-anchored tasks that were never done, do we keep every missed instance as a row (auditable) or collapse them into one row with a miss count (usable)?

topic: Sub-daily cadence ('every 2 hours between open and close') is a window+frequency model, not a recurrence rule
detail: RRULE can technically express this via FREQ=HOURLY;INTERVAL=2 or FREQ=DAILY;BYHOUR=10,12,14,16,18,20, but both bind the times to fixed clock hours from DTSTART. Neither can say 'between whenever we open today and whenever we close today'. Operational tools (Operandio, Jolt) instead model a schedule with start and due times and let staff complete within it, and hospitality checks are configured as per-shift frequencies rather than clock recurrences.
why_it_matters: A toilet check every 2 hours between open and close is genuinely a different shape from a calendar recurrence: the count of instances depends on the day's trading hours, which vary by day of week and by season. Encoding it as BYHOUR forces you to duplicate the rule per opening pattern and re-edit every rule when hours change.
decision_needed: Do we model intra-day cadence as (interval + bounded by the day's actual open/close from the trading-hours table) rather than as fixed clock times?

topic: 'Roughly weekly, not on a fixed day' has no calendar representation at all
detail: RRULE requires you to name the day (BYDAY=FR) or accept the DTSTART weekday. There is no 'about every 7 days, ±2, any day'. The available representations are: completion-anchored with an interval (Grocy dynamic-regular, period 7), or a due window (due between day 5 and day 9 from last completion) — i.e. an interval plus a tolerance, which is a two-number model, not a rule string.
why_it_matters: This class of task is extremely common in a pub (change the barrel line clean, restock the cellar, check the bin store) and is the one most likely to be forced into a fake fixed-day rule, which then generates fake overdue noise every week the team did it on Thursday instead of Wednesday.
decision_needed: Do we add a tolerance/slack field (interval_days + tolerance_days) so 'roughly weekly' is first-class rather than approximated?

topic: Meter-based PM is the third anchor, and it generalises beyond machinery
detail: CMMS tools schedule on usage thresholds — every 5,000 miles, every 500 run-hours — and these milestones stay fixed even when the previous work order was completed early or late. Combined plans fire "when the time interval or usage threshold is reached, whichever comes first".
why_it_matters: A pub has meters even without machines: covers served, kegs pulled, cleans since last descale, days since last deep clean. Modelling 'whichever comes first' as a general OR over triggers means glasswasher descaling can be 'every 3 months OR every 2,000 cycles' without a bespoke feature. The catch: 'whichever comes first' needs a clear reset rule on completion or both counters drift out of step.
decision_needed: Do we want a counter/meter trigger at all in v1, or is time-only sufficient until a concrete case (glasswasher, cellar cooler) demands it?

topic: Due-date rollover: the option to make a task incapable of being overdue
detail: Grocy ships a "Due date rollover" flag: when enabled "the chore can never be overdue, the due date will shift forward each day when due". It is a distinct concept from both anchors — the task stays perpetually due-today until someone does it.
why_it_matters: This is the honest model for tasks where lateness is meaningless but the task must not disappear (e.g. 'replace the noticeboard poster'). It also prevents the overdue list becoming a graveyard that staff learn to ignore — the single biggest failure mode of checklist software.
decision_needed: Which tasks are rollover (nag forever, never overdue) vs strict (one shot, then missed)?

topic: Overdue, missed and skipped are three different states and conflating them destroys the audit trail
detail: Checklist tools distinguish results as pass, fail, not applicable, skipped, or needs follow-up. Overdue = past due, still actionable. Missed = the window closed unactioned, no longer completable. Skipped = a human deliberately declined it, ideally with a reason. Chore Helper never auto-completes — chores go to a negative day count and stay visible until someone calls complete.
why_it_matters: Compliance value lives entirely in this distinction. 'Toilet check missed at 21:00' and 'toilet check skipped at 21:00 — venue closed early for a private event' are the same absence of a tick but opposite facts in an EHO inspection. Auto-expiring a missed task with no record is the worst option: it looks clean and proves nothing.
decision_needed: Can staff skip with a mandatory reason, and does a skip satisfy the recurrence (advance the next due date) or not?

topic: 'Due at' vs 'due by' are different contracts and need different UI and alerting
detail: Operandio models tasks with both start and due times — staff are told which jobs they must complete "and at what time they're due", i.e. a window rather than an instant. A 'due at' task (open-up temperature check at 11:00) is a point obligation; a 'due by' task (bin store tidied by close) is a deadline over a window.
why_it_matters: They alert differently. 'Due at' should nag near the instant and mark late almost immediately — its value is the timestamp. 'Due by' should be silent until near the deadline. Using one model for both either spams staff all afternoon or fails to catch a check done 3 hours off-schedule.
decision_needed: Do we store window_start and due_at separately (supports both shapes) or just due_at plus a grace period (simpler, can't express a real window)?

topic: Grace periods are a lie about lateness unless the true timestamp is kept
detail: A grace period is a display/alerting concession: the task is late but we're not going to say so yet. The dangerous implementation is one that mutates the due date or records the completion as on-time. The safe implementation records the actual completion timestamp, computes lateness truthfully, and applies the grace only to notification and to the red/amber state.
why_it_matters: For anything with a compliance flavour, a grace period that rewrites history means the audit trail can never answer 'was this actually done on time?'. It also compounds with floating intervals: if grace shifts the recorded completion, the next due date is computed from a fictional date.
decision_needed: Is grace strictly presentational (never touches stored completed_at or the next-due calculation)?

topic: The business day must be a configurable boundary, not midnight
detail: Time-and-attendance systems solve the past-midnight problem with an explicit Day Boundary setting — moved from midnight "to a time when shifts are completed", which stretches the period to include after-midnight hours. Scheduling tools take the parallel approach of attributing a midnight-crossing shift to a daypart by its start. Financial systems do the same thing with a trade-date roll at 20:00 rather than midnight.
why_it_matters: A 00:30 close means the last toilet check of Friday night happens on Saturday by the calendar. If business_day is derived as (timestamp - boundary_offset)::date rather than timestamp::date, then close-down tasks, the day's checklist completion rate, and the manager's morning report all attribute correctly with no special-casing anywhere else. Get this wrong and every report is quietly off by one for the busiest hours of the week.
decision_needed: Do we adopt a single configurable business-day start (e.g. 06:00 London) applied uniformly to task attribution and reporting, and store business_date as a derived column?

topic: Tasks due on a closed day need a stated policy: skip, defer, or pull forward
detail: Trading systems simply do not report activity on non-business days — the day is skipped entirely. Scheduling systems more often defer to the next open day. RRULE itself has no notion of a closed day; the only standard tool is EXDATE, which removes the occurrence and does not move it.
why_it_matters: The right answer differs per task and cannot be a global setting. A daily toilet check on a closed Monday should vanish (skip — there were no customers). A weekly cellar clean falling on a closed Monday should move to Tuesday (defer — the work still needs doing). A monthly stock count due on a closed day probably should pull backward to the last open day (deadline-driven). Picking one policy for all three produces either phantom overdue tasks or silently dropped work.
decision_needed: Do we add a per-task closed_day_policy of skip / defer_forward / pull_backward, defaulting to skip for daily operational checks and defer_forward for periodic ones?

topic: Seasonal and conditional tasks: RRULE handles the seasonal case, not the conditional one
detail: RRULE covers 'Autumn/Winter only' cleanly — FREQ=WEEKLY;BYMONTH=10,11,12,1,2,3 — and 'Fri-Sat only' with BYDAY=FR,SA, and the two compose. What it cannot express is a predicate over external state: only when the fire is lit, only when the garden is open, only when covers exceeded 200, only when it rained.
why_it_matters: Seasonal is a filter on the generator (cheap, static, safe to precompute). Conditional is a runtime predicate that can only be evaluated on the day itself. Treating them the same forces either the loss of conditional tasks or the abandonment of any lookahead. The pragmatic split: generate seasonally-filtered instances ahead of time, and evaluate conditional predicates at instance-activation time, letting an instance be born already 'not applicable'.
decision_needed: Do conditional tasks appear and then get marked N/A, or never appear at all? The former is auditable ('we considered it, it didn't apply'), the latter is cleaner for staff.

topic: Full materialisation of instances is the standard mistake
detail: The prevailing design guidance is not to store individual recurring instances as rows: materialised approaches consume space, make updates cumbersome and slow, complicate exception handling — especially rescheduling — and are outright unworkable if you want indefinite forward visibility. The alternative is storing the pattern and expanding on read.
why_it_matters: The pain is concentrated in edits, not storage. Change 'every Tuesday' to 'every Wednesday' on a fully materialised series and you must decide what happens to every already-generated future row, including any that were individually edited, reassigned or annotated. That migration logic is where these systems rot.
decision_needed: None — this is a well-established constraint. It shapes the choice below.

topic: The hybrid — pattern plus selectively materialised instances — is what mature tools converge on
detail: TaskNotes stores an RRULE string with DTSTART and computes the next occurrence dynamically, while allowing optional materialised occurrence notes for a specific date when that occurrence needs its own checklist, status, time tracking or content. MaintainX achieves the same via a configurable scheduling horizon that "configures how far ahead you want to plan work orders" — materialise only inside the horizon, compute beyond it.
why_it_matters: This gets both properties: infinite cheap forward visibility, and a real durable row the moment an instance acquires state (a tick, a photo, a temperature reading, an assignee, a skip reason). The rule is simple and enforceable — an instance is materialised on first write, never on read.
decision_needed: Do we adopt materialise-on-first-write with a rolling horizon (e.g. 14 days) for instances that need to be visible and assignable before anyone touches them?

topic: Completion-anchored tasks cannot be computed on the fly and must materialise — this splits the architecture
detail: An 'every-N' series is a pure function of the pattern, so occurrence #500 is computable today. An 'after-N' series is a fold over history: the next due date exists only once the previous completion exists. Grocy's dynamic-regular therefore has exactly one due date at any moment — there is nothing to forecast. Chore Helper's calendar reflects this too: it shows projected future due dates for regular chores, but manual chores "show limited forecasting since they lack predictable recurrence patterns".
why_it_matters: This is the deepest structural consequence in the whole design. The two anchor modes are not two values of one column — they are two different computational shapes. Calendar-anchored tasks support a forward calendar view; completion-anchored tasks fundamentally cannot, and any UI promising 'your next 4 weeks' will be lying about them.
decision_needed: Are we prepared for completion-anchored tasks to be absent from forward-looking calendar views, or do we need a projected/provisional instance concept that assumes on-time completion?

topic: Rescheduling one instance vs editing the series — the two-axis edit problem
detail: RFC 5545 solves this with RECURRENCE-ID plus THISANDFUTURE/THISANDPRIOR range semantics, and RDATE/EXDATE for one-off additions and removals. Grocy takes the pragmatic route with a "Reschedule next execution" action in the chore's context menu that moves only the next occurrence, leaving the pattern untouched.
why_it_matters: Users need four distinct edits — this one, this and future, the whole series, and just skip this one — and every product that ships only 'edit' regrets it. If instances are materialised, a per-instance override is a column; if computed, it's an exception row keyed by occurrence date. Either way it has to be designed in, because retrofitting exceptions to a pure-pattern model is a rewrite.
decision_needed: Do we support 'this instance only' overrides in v1, or accept that any change hits the whole series?

topic: Timezone and DST: store the rule in local time, never in UTC
detail: RFC 5545 expands recurrence in the timezone of DTSTART. A task defined as 'every day at 11:00' must expand as 11:00 local on every date, which means the UTC instant moves by an hour twice a year. Storing the anchor in UTC and expanding produces a check that silently becomes 10:00 or 12:00 across the DST boundary. This project already has the related scar tissue: manager alerts printed raw UTC and the bug was invisible on a London laptop.
why_it_matters: Europe/London is UTC+0 for part of the year, which makes every DST bug undetectable in local testing and guarantees it ships. Combined with the business-day boundary, the 01:00 BST→GMT transition also creates a genuinely ambiguous local hour — a nightly close-down task anchored at 01:30 happens twice or not at all.
decision_needed: None — expand in Europe/London from a local-time anchor, store instances with both the local wall time and the resolved instant, and test with TZ=UTC.

topic: Rest days / minimum-gap constraints are a separate layer from the recurrence rule
detail: Beyond the generator, tools need to suppress or shift occurrences that land badly — Grocy's daily/weekly types let you restrict to specific weekdays or days of month, but the general shape is a post-filter over generated occurrences rather than a property of the rule itself.
why_it_matters: Layering (generate → filter by season/weekday → apply closed-day policy → apply minimum-gap) keeps each concern independently testable and lets the same filters apply to both anchor modes. Folding them into a single mega-rule column means every new constraint is a schema change.
decision_needed: Do we model recurrence as a pipeline (generator + ordered filters) rather than a single rule expression?

topic: Notification timing must derive from the window, not from the due instant
detail: Operational tools alert on overdue, out-of-range and incomplete tasks, and the common complaint pattern across these products is alert fatigue when every instance of a high-frequency task nags. A toilet check every 2 hours across a 14-hour trading day is 7 instances; at one reminder each plus one overdue each, that's 14 notifications per day for one task.
why_it_matters: High-frequency intra-day tasks and low-frequency periodic tasks need opposite notification defaults, and the frequency itself is the signal. Sensible rule: notify per instance only for low-frequency tasks; for intra-day cadences, notify on the shift-level rollup ('3 of 7 toilet checks outstanding') and escalate only on a miss.
decision_needed: Do notifications attach to instances (simple, noisy) or to a shift-level rollup for sub-daily tasks (quieter, more code)?

topic: What Trail actually scores (the closest comparator)
detail: Trail (UK hospitality checklist app) scores SITES, not individuals. Each task is scored on timeliness — completed on time / early / late / missed — and the results are averaged into a daily site score, then rolled up to Area and Company level and emailed round daily. Audits are scored separately, with per-option point values, and questions with a blank score are excluded from the total. Scores can be filtered by task, tag or site to find bad task schedules.
why_it_matters: The market-leading UK hospitality tool deliberately measures punctuality of completion at site level. It is a schedule-quality diagnostic ("this task is always missed at 4pm on Sundays — fix the schedule") more than a people-judging tool. If we score individuals we are doing something Trail chose not to do.
decision_needed: Do we score the site/shift (Trail's model) or the individual? Recommendation: score the shift/site first — it gives the same operational signal without the employment-law and gaming exposure.

topic: Zenput/Crunchtime measures completion rate, not quality
detail: Crunchtime Ops Execution (formerly Zenput) sells "real-time visibility into task completion rates and cross-store performance", with automated follow-up tasks and resolution monitoring when something fails. Claimed outcomes are ~20% improvement in audit scores and ~24% fewer compliance issues. Note the honesty of the framing: the metric is completion rate — a proxy — and the value comes from the follow-up loop, not the number.
why_it_matters: The score is only the trigger. The thing that changes outcomes is the corrective-action workflow attached to a failed item. A score with no follow-up task is decoration.
decision_needed: Does every failed/missed item generate a follow-up action with an owner, or does it just dent a number?

topic: Jolt's anti-pencil-whipping model is proof-per-item, not proof-per-checklist
detail: Jolt lets each individual list item demand its own verification: a photo of the finished job, a QR scan at the location, a typed value, or a training video watched. Over 18 item types. Tasks are stamped with time and name automatically. The design principle: "employees cannot check a task off as complete unless the QR code is scanned."
why_it_matters: Proof is applied selectively to the items that matter, not to everything. Requiring a photo on all 30 items makes the list unusable; requiring it on the 3 that get faked makes it honest.
decision_needed: Which specific tasks warrant hard proof (photo/scan/value) versus a plain tick? Recommendation: only the killer items — see below.

topic: Yoobic-style gamification rewards learning, not compliance — and that distinction is deliberate
detail: Yoobic applies points, badges, real-time leaderboards, head-to-head "battles" and store-vs-store competition to TRAINING and microlearning modules — courses and quizzes completed. Reported results include Michaels lifting store engagement from 30% to 80-90% and cutting voluntary turnover 24%. The gamification sits on the learning layer, not the safety-checklist layer.
why_it_matters: Gamifying voluntary skill-building is low-risk: faking it gains you nothing real. Gamifying a food-safety checklist creates a direct incentive to tick without doing. Copying the leaderboard onto the wrong layer inverts its effect.
decision_needed: If we gamify, do we gamify training/optional work (safe) or mandatory compliance tasks (dangerous)? Recommendation: the former only.

topic: Pencil-whipping is the industry's named, chronic failure mode
detail: "Pencil-whipping" — hurriedly checking off items without doing or verifying them — is described across the sector (Crunchtime, ComplianceMate, PathSpot, Jolt) as a chronic problem in restaurant food safety, specifically the falsification of temperature and inspection logs. Digitising alone does not fix it: a digital solution only protects against falsified entries "if it's designed to do so."
why_it_matters: Introducing a score without proof mechanics does not reduce pencil-whipping — it increases the payoff for it. You have created a reason to fake the data where previously there was only apathy.
decision_needed: Are we prepared to accept that a scored checklist with no proof requirements will produce prettier data and identical reality?

topic: Goodhart's Law with hard evidence: the proxy improves while reality doesn't
detail: NHS England's four-hour A&E target (budget penalties for missing it) produced documented systematic gaming — patients held in ambulances outside the door so the four-hour clock never started, and patients reclassified out of the target category; a 2009 Public Accounts Committee inquiry documented widespread manipulation. Wells Fargo's cross-sell targets drove forged signatures and millions of unauthorised accounts (SEC 2020 order, "gaming practices" 2002-2016). Same structure every time: proxy + consequences → agents optimise the proxy → link to the real goal breaks.
why_it_matters: The distortion is proportional to the consequence attached. A task score used for information behaves differently from the identical score used for rotas, pay or discipline. The moment it carries a consequence, staff will optimise the score, not the pub.
decision_needed: Will the score ever be attached to a consequence (rota hours, bonus, disciplinary)? Recommendation: keep it consequence-free and diagnostic. If it must carry weight, it needs every anti-gaming control below plus human review.

topic: Checklists can be fully adopted and still change nothing — the Ontario study
detail: Ontario mandated the WHO Surgical Safety Checklist across the province. The NEJM study (Urbach et al., 2014) found no significant improvement in patient outcomes at population level, in contrast to the earlier trials. A key explanation: hospitals over-reported compliance, and teams "embraced checklists and safety culture grudgingly rather than enthusiastically." Reported use and actual use diverged.
why_it_matters: This is the single most important evidence point. Near-100% reported completion is exactly what a compliance-theatre system produces. High scores are as consistent with grudging fakery as with excellence, and the score itself cannot tell you which you have.
decision_needed: How will we distinguish a genuine 100% from a faked 100%? Recommendation: randomised physical spot-audits, and treat divergence between audit and self-report as the real metric.

topic: Gawande's design rules: 5-9 items, 60-90 seconds, killer items only
detail: The Checklist Manifesto's operating constraints: keep the list to five to nine items; focus on the "killer items" — those most dangerous to skip and most often overlooked; after about sixty to ninety seconds at a pause point the checklist becomes a distraction from the actual work.
why_it_matters: Most hospitality task lists are 25-40 items, which is a guaranteed pencil-whip by construction. If it can't be done in 90 seconds it will be batch-ticked. Cutting the list is itself an anti-gaming control, and probably the highest-leverage one.
decision_needed: Are we willing to delete most of the existing checklist items to get to 5-9 killer items per pause point? Recommendation: yes — a short honest list beats a long fictional one.

topic: Pause points, and read-do vs do-confirm
detail: Gawande: a checklist needs a defined pause point at which it is used (unless the trigger is self-evident, like a warning light). READ-DO = read the item, then do it, recipe-style. DO-CONFIRM = do the work from memory/flow, then pause and confirm each item was done.
why_it_matters: This maps directly onto the batch-completion problem. Opening prep is READ-DO and must be ticked live, item by item. A close-down verification is legitimately DO-CONFIRM and is genuinely completed in one burst at the end — so a system that flags "all ticked within 60 seconds" as fraud will produce false accusations against people doing it correctly.
decision_needed: Which of our lists are read-do (live ticking expected) and which are do-confirm (burst completion is correct)? The anti-gaming rules must differ per list type.

topic: Time-stamping is the baseline control, and it is cheap
detail: Standard practice across Jolt, ComplianceMate and Trail: automatic time-and-date stamps plus name stamps on every entry, retained for audit and trend analysis. Trail's whole score is built on stamp-versus-schedule comparison (early/on time/late/missed).
why_it_matters: Timestamps cost nothing to add and are the raw material for every other control — batch detection, backdating blocks, schedule diagnosis. Without them, no score is defensible.

topic: Detecting batch-completion at end of shift
detail: The signature of end-of-shift batch-ticking: 20 items stamped within a two-minute window, hours after their scheduled pause point. The controls used in practice are (a) blocking completion of a task before its window opens (no future/pre-completion) and before its dependencies, (b) blocking or flagging retrospective completion after the window closes, and (c) requiring a captured VALUE (a temperature, a reading, a count) rather than a tick — a value cannot be plausibly invented for 20 items in 90 seconds, and touchless temperature capture exists specifically to remove the human from that loop.
why_it_matters: "Require a value not a tick" is the highest-value control per unit of friction. It converts a binary tick into evidence with internal consistency you can check (fridge temps that never vary at all are as suspicious as ones that are wildly out).
decision_needed: Do we block late completion outright, or allow it with a mandatory reason? Recommendation: allow with a reason and flag it — hard blocks push staff into logging on someone else's account, which destroys the data entirely.

topic: Location proof (QR/NFC/geofence) works but is legally the heaviest control
detail: QR codes fixed at the task location, or NFC tags, force physical presence: the item cannot be ticked without a scan at the point of work. Jolt and Miratag both build on this. Geofencing/GPS does the same but continuously. Under UK GDPR, ICO expects documented evidence that less intrusive alternatives were considered and rejected for specific, articulable reasons; a DPIA is typically required for systematic location collection; continuous background tracking should be avoided in favour of event-based collection (e.g. only at clock-in/out).
why_it_matters: A QR/NFC scan is a discrete, task-scoped presence proof and is far easier to justify than a geofence, which collects location continuously and captures far more than the task. The QR gets you ~all the anti-gaming benefit at ~none of the legal risk.
decision_needed: QR/NFC scan-at-location, or geofence? Recommendation: QR/NFC only. Geofencing a single-site pub is disproportionate — the staff are demonstrably on site or they wouldn't be working.

topic: Photo evidence: deterrent effect is mostly psychological
detail: The stated mechanic across vendors: requiring a mobile photo upload means employees know they are creating digital evidence, which makes them less likely to falsify. Jolt frames photo-proof and QR-scan proof as the specific controls that "eliminate pencil-whipping" on critical tasks.
why_it_matters: Note what this is and isn't. Nobody claims managers review every photo — the deterrent is the possibility of review, not review itself. That means a photo requirement only works if spot-checks are actually done sometimes and staff know it. An unreviewed photo archive is a slow way to store nothing. Also: photos of a pub interior will capture staff and customers — that's personal data with its own retention obligation.
decision_needed: Who reviews photos, how often, and how long are they retained? Recommendation: random sampling, short retention (e.g. 30 days), and say so openly.

topic: Randomised spot audits are the only external check on score validity
detail: The Ontario finding — hospitals over-reported compliance — was only detectable because someone measured actual use independently of self-report. Trail's own audit module is scored separately from task completion, which preserves exactly this independence.
why_it_matters: Self-reported completion validates nothing about itself. The genuinely informative number is the gap between what the checklist says and what an unannounced physical check finds. That gap is the honest measure of the system, and it should be tracked at site level — never as a gotcha against the individual who happened to be on shift when the auditor walked in.
decision_needed: Will someone actually walk the floor unannounced? If not, the whole scoring apparatus is unverifiable.

topic: UK law changed in February 2026 — Article 22 is now 22A-22D
detail: Article 22 of the UK GDPR was replaced by new Articles 22A-22D on 5 February 2026 under section 80 of the Data (Use and Access) Act 2025. Since then, controllers making solely or predominantly automated decisions with legal or similarly significant effects must: provide specified information, offer human review on request, accept representations from the individual, and allow the decision to be contested. Employment decisions — performance assessment, promotion, disciplinary action, termination — are the canonical examples that trigger this.
why_it_matters: This is current, not prospective, and most guidance written before Feb 2026 is now out of date. If a task score ever drives a disciplinary step, a rota reduction or a dismissal without a human genuinely in the loop, we are squarely inside 22A-22D. Note the widened wording: "predominantly" automated, not just "solely" — a manager who rubber-stamps the dashboard is still caught.
decision_needed: Will the score ever feed a decision with significant effect on someone's job? Recommendation: write into the policy that it cannot, and that it is an input to a conversation only.

topic: "Meaningful human involvement" is a real bar, not a signature box
detail: The reviewer must have the authority and capability to change the decision, and must consider all relevant data including context the individual has supplied. "A superficial or rubber-stamp review does not count as human involvement." Enforcement is live: Italy's Garante fined Foodinho €5m for algorithmically managing shifts, assigning orders and deactivating 35,000+ riders' accounts with no human intervention mechanism and no way to contest.
why_it_matters: The Foodinho case is the exact shape of what a task-completion score could become: an algorithm that quietly influences who gets shifts. The safeguard isn't a human in the org chart — it's a human who could and sometimes does overturn the number.
decision_needed: Can a manager overturn a score, and is the override logged with a reason? Recommendation: yes to both — the override log is also your best evidence that the score is broken where it's overridden a lot.

topic: ICO's 2023 monitoring guidance: the incomplete-picture trap
detail: ICO published final guidance on monitoring workers on 3 October 2023. Employers must identify one of the six lawful bases up front; monitoring must take account of workers' expectations, must not be excessive, and must be transparent wherever possible; a DPIA is needed where monitoring is likely to be high risk. The ICO's own worked example: monitoring how long workers spend in a case management system to assess performance is "unfair and inadequate" UNLESS the employer takes into account the work done outside the system.
why_it_matters: That example is our exact scenario. A member of staff who spends the shift calming an angry table, fixing a cellar problem and covering a no-show may score badly on ticks while being the best person on the floor. Scoring only what the app can see, and calling it performance, is the specific thing the ICO names as unfair.
decision_needed: Does the score get presented as "performance", or as "checklist completion" — a narrow, named, partial measure? Recommendation: the latter, and never label a column "performance".

topic: Part-timers vs full-timers: pro rata or don't compare at all
detail: The Part-time Workers (Prevention of Less Favourable Treatment) Regulations 2000 require that part-timers not be treated less favourably than comparable full-timers, with the pro rata principle applied unless inappropriate, and cover performance-related pay and bonuses explicitly. Different treatment needs objective justification — a legitimate aim pursued by proportionate means.
why_it_matters: Any raw count of tasks completed automatically ranks full-timers above part-timers, and any league table built on it is structurally unfair. Rate-based scoring (completed ÷ assigned-while-rostered) is the minimum fix. But rates are noisy on small denominators: someone doing six shifts a month who misses two tasks looks catastrophic next to a full-timer who missed six out of 300.
decision_needed: Do we suppress individual scores below a minimum task volume? Recommendation: yes — set a floor (e.g. no individual figure below ~30 assigned tasks in the period) and show the count alongside the rate always.

topic: Whoever's on shift ≠ whoever's responsible
detail: The controllability principle in performance measurement: accountability and authority must be aligned, and measures must be set on factors within the individual's control, or motivation and effectiveness both degrade. Fairness is the mediating mechanism between controllability and performance.
why_it_matters: In a pub, tasks land on whoever is physically there. If the delivery is late, the cellar check can't be done — and the person on shift eats the miss. If a task was never assigned to a named person, nobody can be scored on it without violating controllability. This is the most likely way a scoring system quietly becomes hated: people are penalised for things they could not have done.
decision_needed: Is every scored task explicitly assigned to a named person at the time it falls due? If not, it must be scored to the shift, not to a person.

topic: Scoring team-owned tasks: score the shift, and use a blocked/N-A state
detail: Team rewards promote collaboration but risk free-riding; individual incentives raise accountability but discourage teamwork. Team-based rewards work best in groups of ten or fewer, where mutual visibility makes free-riding hard to hide. Peer-evaluation is the standard academic fix for apportioning credit within a team.
why_it_matters: A pub shift is a small team with high mutual visibility — the exact condition where shift-level scoring is defensible and free-riding is self-policing. Practical model: (1) default unit of scoring is the SHIFT, not the person; (2) a task becomes person-scored only if explicitly claimed/assigned to a named individual before it falls due; (3) every task needs a third state beyond done/missed — "blocked" or "not applicable", with a reason — so a genuinely impossible task doesn't score as a miss; (4) a shift's score is visible to that shift, not published in a cross-site league table of individuals.
decision_needed: Shift-level scoring with opt-in individual attribution — agreed? Recommendation: yes. It matches the operational reality, sidesteps the part-time fairness problem, keeps us out of Art. 22A territory, and mirrors what Trail actually does.

topic: The reason-code is worth more than the score
detail: Trail's design intent is telling: low scores highlighted by site or day let you "drill down to fix task schedules or work on staff training." The score's stated purpose is finding broken schedules first, and training needs second — not ranking people.
why_it_matters: A missed task has causes: bad schedule, understaffed shift, task genuinely impossible, or someone didn't do it. Only the last is a people problem, and it's usually the rarest. If the system captures a reason on every miss, the score becomes a diagnostic instrument. If it doesn't, every miss defaults to blaming a person — and staff will learn to tick rather than explain, which is exactly the behaviour we were trying to prevent.
decision_needed: Do we require a reason code on every missed/blocked task? Recommendation: yes, from a short fixed list plus free text. This single feature does more for data quality than the score does.

topic: Shared login attribution (manager@ device)
detail: If the whole team works from one shared tablet signed in as a generic account, every completion writes the same user_id and the scoring system measures the device, not a person. Realistic options: (a) require a per-person PIN/4-digit code on the shared device at the moment of completion (an 'acting_as' identity separate from the auth session), reusing the existing timeclock kiosk pattern which already identifies individuals without a Supabase session; (b) auto-attribute to whoever is currently clocked in — works only when exactly one person is on; (c) if multiple are clocked in, present a 'who are you?' picker of currently-clocked-in staff, default to last used, timeout back to unattributed; (d) accept an 'unattributed' bucket and exclude those from personal scores but still count them for venue completion. Recommendation: PIN/picker gated on clocked-in staff, with an explicit 'unattributed' fallback rather than silently crediting the shared account.
why_it_matters: Attribution is the foundation of the whole scoring feature. If it's wrong, every score, escalation email and spot-check is aimed at the wrong person, and staff will lose trust in the system permanently after the first bad accusation.
