decision_needed: Do you want a per-person PIN on the shared device, or is 'whoever is clocked in' good enough — and what happens when three people are clocked in at once?

topic: Completion by someone not clocked in
detail: A staff member does the beer line clean before clocking in, or the manager ticks things off from home. Options: (a) block completion unless clocked in — clean data, but staff will simply clock in to tick a box, corrupting time/pay data, which is far worse; (b) allow it, record it, and flag it as 'completed off-shift' for the manager to review; (c) allow it but exclude from personal score; (d) allow it and let it silently count. Recommendation: (b) — allow and flag, never block. The checklist must never become a reason to falsify clock records.
why_it_matters: Blocking on clock state creates a perverse incentive to fake timesheets, which touches pay and HMRC records. It converts a low-stakes admin problem into a payroll integrity problem.
decision_needed: Should off-shift completions count towards a personal score, or only towards venue completion?

topic: Clock out then return
detail: Someone clocks out at 22:45, then comes back at 23:10 to help with close-down and ticks two tasks. Their session is closed, so 'tasks done during my shift' misses them, and the clock-out warning already fired. Options: (a) attribute by wall-clock time to the nearest/most recent shift for that person that day; (b) attribute to the person regardless of shift, and only use shift data for the warning trigger; (c) re-open the shift. Recommendation: (b) — decouple attribution (person + business day) from shift boundaries entirely; use shifts only to decide when to show the clock-out warning.
why_it_matters: Coupling task records to open shift rows means real work vanishes from the record whenever the timing is untidy, which is most nights.

topic: Two people complete the same task
detail: Race: two devices submit the same instance within seconds, or one person ticks it not seeing the other already did. Options: (a) unique constraint on (instance_id) so first write wins and the second gets a friendly 'already done by Sam at 21:14' rather than an error; (b) allow multiple completion rows and take the first for scoring, showing the rest as corroborating; (c) last write wins — bad, it silently rewrites who did it. Recommendation: (a), with a DB-level unique constraint (not just an app check) and an explicit 'already completed' UI state that refreshes rather than throws.
why_it_matters: Without a DB constraint this is a real race on a busy close-down with three phones open on the same list, and 'last write wins' means credit goes to whoever tapped slowest.
decision_needed: If two people genuinely both did it, do you want to record both or just the first?

topic: One person completes another person's assigned task
detail: The task is assigned to the cellar person but the duty manager does it. Options: (a) record completed_by separately from assigned_to and score the doer, not the assignee; (b) score the assignee (they're accountable) and note who actually did it; (c) require a 'covering for X' reason. Recommendation: (a) plus display both — score credit follows the doer, but the assignee's list shows it cleared. Never mark the assignee as 'missed' for something that was completed by someone else.
why_it_matters: Pubs run on covering for each other. A system that penalises the person whose name was on the list when the job actually got done will be resented and gamed within a week.
decision_needed: When someone else does your task, do you get credit, no credit, or a neutral 'covered' outcome?

topic: Late completion — done or missed?
detail: A task due by 18:00 is ticked at 19:40. Options: (a) binary — anything after the deadline is missed, even if completed (harsh, and kills the incentive to do it late); (b) three states: on_time / late / missed, where late still counts for venue completion but scores lower; (c) grace window (e.g. 30 min) then late; (d) manager decides case by case. Recommendation: (b) with a grace window — 'late' must always beat 'missed', or you teach staff not to bother once the clock passes.
why_it_matters: If late = missed, the rational move for a staff member at 19:40 is to skip the task entirely. The scoring model directly shapes behaviour on the floor.
decision_needed: Confirm the three-state model and the grace window length (recommend 30 minutes).

topic: Who adjudicates a disputed miss
detail: A staff member says they did it but the phone died / they forgot to tick. Options: (a) manager can retro-complete an instance with a reason and an audit record, marking it 'completed retrospectively — recorded by manager'; (b) no retro-edits at all (pure integrity, guaranteed staff resentment); (c) self-serve retro-tick within N hours. Recommendation: (a) — manager-only override, always audit-logged with the original state preserved, never a silent rewrite.
why_it_matters: Every checklist system in the real world needs an override. Building one without it means the data becomes known-wrong and everyone ignores it; building it without an audit trail means the score is meaningless.
decision_needed: Should staff be able to retro-tick their own missed items within a short window, or is that manager-only?

topic: Pub closed — instances shouldn't count as missed
detail: Christmas Day, a private hire, a burst pipe, or a snow day. If the generator blindly creates instances, everyone gets a wall of red and a scoring hit. Options: (a) a venue closure calendar that suppresses instance generation for closed days; (b) generate but auto-void with reason='closed'; (c) manager bulk 'mark day as closed — void outstanding'. Recommendation: (a) as the primary, plus (c) as the escape hatch for unplanned closures declared after the fact (backdated voiding of that day's open instances). Voided instances must be excluded from both venue and personal scores, not counted as zero.
why_it_matters: One unhandled closed day poisons the score for every person and destroys the credibility of the whole report, and it will happen within the first month.
decision_needed: Is there an existing venue-closure/opening-hours source of truth to key off, or does this need a new closure calendar?

topic: Partial closure / private hire
detail: The pub is open for a private function but the kitchen is shut, so kitchen tasks don't apply while bar tasks do. Blanket day-level closure is too coarse. Options: (a) closure scoped to an area/category (kitchen closed, bar open); (b) all-or-nothing day closure; (c) manual per-instance voiding. Recommendation: (a) — closure applies at the checklist/area level, because the realistic case is 'kitchen shut Mondays', not 'pub shut'.
why_it_matters: A day-level-only closure model forces staff to either void 20 items by hand or accept a false miss, and they will choose to accept the false miss and stop believing the score.
decision_needed: Do closures need to be per-area (kitchen/bar/cellar) from day one, or is whole-venue enough for v1?

topic: Staff illness / nobody on to do it
detail: The person who normally does the cellar check is off sick and nobody covers. Options: (a) it's a genuine miss and should show as one — the venue score is the honest picture; (b) auto-void if nobody with that role was clocked in; (c) miss for the venue, but no personal score hit for anyone. Recommendation: (c) — the venue metric must stay honest (the job didn't happen), but nobody who wasn't there should be penalised personally. This means venue completion and personal scores need different denominators.
why_it_matters: Conflating 'the pub missed it' with 'a person missed it' is the single biggest way this system will produce unfair scores.
decision_needed: Confirm venue score and personal score are computed from different denominators.

topic: Shift crossing midnight — which business day owns a 00:30 task?
detail: Close-down runs to 01:00. A calendar-date model puts those tasks on the next day, splitting one shift's work across two reports and making Tuesday look bad for Monday night's close. Options: (a) a business-day boundary (e.g. 05:00 London) so anything before 05:00 belongs to the previous business day; (b) attribute to the business day of the shift the person was on; (c) leave it calendar-based (wrong, but simple). Recommendation: (a) — a single configurable business-day cutoff constant used by instance generation, scoring and reporting alike. Must use the existing London-timezone dateUtils, never raw new Date().
why_it_matters: Get this wrong and every close-down task lands on the wrong day forever, and no amount of UI polish will make the report believable.
decision_needed: Confirm the business-day cutoff time (recommend 05:00 Europe/London).

topic: BST/GMT transitions
detail: The clocks go back and 01:30 happens twice; they go forward and 01:30 doesn't exist. A checklist due at 01:30 on the transition night either generates twice or not at all, and a 'day' is 23 or 25 hours long. Options: (a) store instance due times as UTC timestamps computed once from London local time at generation, and make generation idempotent on (checklist_id, business_date); (b) ignore it and accept two nights a year of odd data. Recommendation: (a) — the idempotency key on business_date is what actually saves you here, regardless of the clock.
why_it_matters: This project already has a documented history of London-timezone defects; a scheduler that generates duplicates on 26 October is a predictable repeat.

topic: Out-of-range temperature reading
detail: A fridge reads 9°C when the range is 1–5°C. Options: (a) accept the reading silently and mark the task complete — worst case, it's a food-safety record showing a known breach with no action; (b) accept but force a mandatory corrective-action note before the task can be marked complete; (c) accept, force a note, AND raise an immediate alert to the duty manager, and keep the instance in an 'open — action required' state until signed off; (d) reject the reading (terrible — teaches staff to type 4°C). Recommendation: (c). Critically, never validate the value in a way that blocks saving it, or staff will simply enter an in-range number.
why_it_matters: This is the one part of the system with legal and food-safety weight (EHO inspection evidence). A design that nudges staff towards falsifying a reading is worse than having no system at all.
decision_needed: Should an out-of-range temp block the checklist from being marked complete until a corrective action is recorded, and who gets alerted immediately?

topic: Temperature reading data quality
detail: Fat-finger entries (45 instead of 4.5), unit confusion, blank readings, and impossible values (-40, 300). Options: (a) hard bounds rejecting physically impossible values with a clear message; (b) a soft 'are you sure?' confirmation for improbable-but-possible values; (c) require the reading as a number, never free text. Recommendation: hard reject impossible, soft confirm improbable, and store the raw entered value alongside the parsed one.
why_it_matters: A single 45°C typo will trigger an alert, an escalation email and a manager's evening. Two of those and the alerts get muted.

topic: Spot-check when the manager isn't in
detail: A spot check is generated for a day nobody senior is working. Options: (a) only generate spot checks for days where a manager is rostered (join to the rota); (b) generate anyway and let it roll over/expire unactioned — which then reads as a failure by the manager; (c) generate and allow any staff member above a threshold to complete it. Recommendation: (a) — key generation off the rota. If it must generate blind, an unactioned spot check should expire as 'not attempted', never as 'failed'.
why_it_matters: Spot checks that expire as failures because nobody was rostered turn the manager's own score into noise and they'll stop looking at it.
decision_needed: Should spot-check generation read the rota to pick a day/person, or generate blind?

topic: Spot-check randomisation — repeats and clustering
detail: True random picks the same fridge three days running and never picks the cellar. Options: (a) pure random (simple, feels broken); (b) weighted random excluding anything checked in the last N days; (c) shuffled bag/round-robin — every item gets checked once before any repeats, with the order shuffled; (d) risk-weighted — high-risk items (temps, allergens) picked more often. Recommendation: (c) plus a risk weighting on top, with a hard 'not checked in the last 7 days' exclusion.
why_it_matters: Coverage is the actual goal, not randomness. Pure random gives worse coverage and looks arbitrary to staff, which undermines the perceived fairness of the whole thing.
decision_needed: Do you want even coverage (round-robin) or genuine unpredictability (pure random)? They trade off.

topic: Staff learn the spot-check pattern
detail: If the check always lands at 15:00, or always the day after the previous one, staff prepare for it and the check measures preparation, not standards. Options: (a) randomise the time-of-day within the shift, not just the item; (b) don't reveal which item is being checked until the manager opens it; (c) vary frequency (some days none, some days two); (d) don't tell staff spot checks exist at all (bad — trust cost outweighs the benefit). Recommendation: (a)+(b)+(c), and be open with staff that spot checks happen; hide only the what and when.
why_it_matters: A predictable audit is theatre. But secrecy about the existence of monitoring is a trust and, arguably, a transparency problem with staff.

topic: Spot check fails — then what?
detail: The manager checks the cellar and it's not done properly, despite it being ticked. Options: (a) record the fail and do nothing else — pointless; (b) record the fail, require a note/photo evidence, and reverse the original completion to 'disputed'; (c) as (b) plus notify the person who ticked it, so they learn; (d) as (c) plus a repeat-fail escalation path into a real HR conversation. Recommendation: (b)+(c) for v1, with the failed spot check carrying a required corrective action. The key design question is whether a fail retroactively changes the original completion record — recommend it does NOT overwrite it, but attaches a linked 'spot check failed' record, preserving both.
why_it_matters: A spot check that ticks a box and then leads nowhere is a waste of the manager's time; a spot check that silently rewrites history destroys the audit trail.
decision_needed: Does a failed spot check cancel the original completion (and the person's score credit for it), or sit alongside it as a separate record?

topic: Spot check disputed — the check itself is wrong
detail: The manager checks at 23:00 and the area is untidy, but it was done properly at 21:00 and used since. The 'fail' is unfair. Options: (a) let the staff member add a response to a failed spot check; (b) require the manager to record evidence (photo/note) at fail time; (c) no dispute mechanism. Recommendation: (a)+(b) — a fail without evidence and without a right of reply is exactly the sort of record that becomes a problem in a grievance.
why_it_matters: These records could be cited in a performance or disciplinary process. Unevidenced, undisputable fails are a genuine employment-relations risk.

topic: Clock-out warning shown to the wrong person
detail: The KP clocks out at 21:00 and gets a wall of outstanding close-down tasks that belong to the bar team and aren't due until 23:30. Options: (a) filter the warning to items assigned to that person or their role/area; (b) filter to items actually due before now; (c) show everything (noise, ignored within days); (d) only warn the designated duty manager / last person out. Recommendation: (a)+(b) combined — warn about my items that are already overdue. Show 'other outstanding items' as read-only context at most, and never block clock-out.
why_it_matters: A warning that is usually irrelevant gets dismissed reflexively within a week, and then it never works even when it matters. And blocking clock-out interferes with pay records.
decision_needed: Should the clock-out warning ever be blocking, or always dismissible? (Recommend always dismissible.)

topic: Clock-out warning for items not yet due
detail: At 20:00 the close-down list is untouched because close-down hasn't happened yet. Warning about it is noise. Options: (a) only warn on items whose due time has passed; (b) warn on items due before the venue closes, since this person won't be back; (c) warn on nothing due later than the person's shift end. Recommendation: (a) as the rule, with (c) as a possible softer 'heads up, these are due after you leave — is someone covering?' prompt only for the duty manager.
why_it_matters: 'Not yet due' is the single biggest source of false warnings, and false warnings are what kill this feature.

topic: Last person out carries everyone's misses
detail: Whoever clocks out last sees every outstanding item and, if the report says 'outstanding at clock-out: Jane', Jane looks responsible for the whole team's misses. Options: (a) never attribute an outstanding item to the person clocking out — attribute the miss to the assignee or to 'venue'; (b) record 'acknowledged outstanding at clock-out' as a distinct, non-punitive event; (c) attribute to the duty manager as accountable (defensible, but they'll fight it). Recommendation: (a)+(b). The warning is an informational prompt, not an assignment of blame — the data model must keep 'saw the warning' and 'was responsible' strictly separate.
why_it_matters: This is the fastest route to the system being seen as unfair, and the last person out is usually the most senior and conscientious person there.
decision_needed: Is the duty manager accountable for the venue's outstanding items at close, or is nobody?

topic: Escalation email about someone else's task
detail: An automated email says 'you missed 4 tasks' but two of them were never that person's, or were covered by someone else. Options: (a) only include items where the person is the assignee AND was clocked in that day; (b) send the manager a venue-level digest instead of emailing individuals at all; (c) send individual emails but require a manager to review/release the batch. Recommendation: (b) for v1 — start with a manager digest and no automated staff emails. Automated accusations aimed at individuals are high-risk and low-value compared with the manager having a one-minute conversation.
why_it_matters: A wrong automated accusation costs far more in trust than the entire feature gains, and it lands in writing, in the employee's inbox, where it stays.
decision_needed: Do you want automated emails to individual staff at all in v1, or only a manager digest? (Recommend manager digest only.)

topic: Escalation email fatigue
detail: A nightly 'you missed things' email becomes wallpaper within two weeks. Options: (a) send only when a threshold is crossed (e.g. 3+ misses, or any food-safety item); (b) weekly digest instead of nightly; (c) suppress if the count is trending down; (d) only escalate on repeat misses of the same item. Recommendation: (a)+(d) — escalate on pattern and severity, not volume. Also: never send a 'well done, 100%' email nightly either; that trains people to ignore the sender.
why_it_matters: The value of an alert is inversely proportional to its frequency. A nightly email guarantees the important one gets ignored.
decision_needed: What threshold justifies an email — recommend any missed food-safety/temperature item, or the same item missed 3 times in 7 days.

topic: Staff member has no email address on file
detail: Many pub staff, especially casual workers, have no email in the record, or a stale one. Options: (a) fall back to SMS (this app already has Twilio) — but an SMS accusation is even more intrusive; (b) silently skip — the escalation just doesn't happen and nobody knows it didn't; (c) fall back to notifying the manager instead, and flag 'could not contact X'; (d) show it in-app on their next login/clock-in. Recommendation: (c)+(d). Never silently skip — a delivery failure must surface somewhere, or you'll believe escalation is working when it isn't.
why_it_matters: Silent non-delivery is the classic failure of notification systems: the report says 'sent', the person never heard, and the manager assumes it was handled.
decision_needed: If a staff member has no email, do you want an SMS, or does it route to the manager?

topic: GDPR and tone of automated performance emails
detail: An automated 'you missed these tasks' email is performance-related personal data processing. Considerations: staff should know the monitoring exists (transparency/Art. 13 — this belongs in a policy or the staff handbook, not buried); the data must be accurate (Art. 5(1)(d)) and correctable, which means a dispute route; it shouldn't be a solely-automated decision with legal/significant effects (Art. 22) — so it must be framed as information for a human manager, not an automatic sanction; retention must be bounded. Tone: 'Here's your checklist summary for Tuesday — 2 items outstanding' reads very differently from 'You failed to complete...'. Options: (a) neutral factual digest to the individual; (b) manager-only, human-relayed; (c) no individual emails. Recommendation: (b), with the monitoring disclosed in writing to staff before go-live.
why_it_matters: This is monitoring of workers. Doing it without telling them, without a correction route, and with an accusatory automated tone is both a legal exposure and a morale problem.
decision_needed: Will you disclose the checklist scoring/monitoring to staff in writing before it goes live? (Strongly recommend yes — it's also the thing that makes it work.)

topic: Checklist item edited after instances exist
detail: Someone changes 'Check fridge temp (1–5°C)' to '(1–8°C)' or rewords a task. If instances point at the live item by FK, yesterday's completed record now displays wording that was never shown to the person who signed it. Options: (a) version the checklist template — instances snapshot the item text, target range and due time at generation, and reference a template_version_id; (b) copy-on-write: editing an item creates a new item and retires the old; (c) live FK with no snapshot (fails audit). Recommendation: (a) — snapshot onto the instance. For a food-safety record, what the person actually attested to must be immutable.
why_it_matters: Without snapshotting, your EHO evidence is retroactively rewritable by anyone with edit rights — which means it isn't evidence.
decision_needed: Confirm instances snapshot item text and thresholds at generation time rather than joining live.

topic: Checklist item deleted mid-period
detail: An item is removed at the end of the week. Do the previous 6 days' completions disappear? Does the score denominator change retroactively so last week's 92% is now 95%? Options: (a) soft-delete/archive only — never hard delete an item that has instances; (b) hard delete and cascade (destroys history); (c) hard delete but orphan-preserve instances via the snapshot. Recommendation: (a) plus (c)'s snapshot — archive the item, keep every instance, and freeze historical scores so a past period's score never changes after the fact.
why_it_matters: Scores that change retroactively when someone edits a template are indefensible, and a manager will notice within a month that last month's number moved.
decision_needed: Should a period's score be frozen/snapshotted once the period closes, or always recomputed live?

topic: Checklist added mid-period
detail: A new item is added on Wednesday. Does it retroactively generate instances for Mon/Tue and instantly show as 2 misses? Options: (a) instances only generate from the item's effective_from date forward — never backfilled; (b) backfill (creates instant phantom misses); (c) ask. Recommendation: (a) — items carry effective_from/effective_to dates and generation is strictly forward-looking.
why_it_matters: Backfilled misses for a task that didn't exist yet is the sort of obviously-wrong data that makes staff dismiss the entire score.

topic: No wifi in the cellar
detail: The cellar is the one place with no signal and it's where the temperature checks are. If the app requires a live request, staff will do the check, walk upstairs, and tick from memory — or not at all. Options: (a) offline-capable PWA with a local queue that syncs on reconnect (this app already registers a service worker); (b) accept it and let staff tick upstairs, with the recorded time being the tick time, not the check time; (c) capture the reading on paper and key it in later (defeats the point); (d) let the person record the check time manually, separate from the submission time. Recommendation: (a) if the budget allows, otherwise (d) — separate 'time of check' from 'time recorded' so the record is honest about the gap.
why_it_matters: If the tool doesn't work where the job is, the record becomes a record of remembering, not of checking — and the timestamps in your food-safety log become fiction.
decision_needed: Is offline support in scope for v1, or do we accept a 'recorded at' vs 'checked at' split?

topic: Offline sync conflicts and duplicates
detail: A queued offline completion syncs 40 minutes later, by which time someone else has completed the same instance, or the item has been voided as 'closed'. Options: (a) server-side reconciliation — first completion by check-time wins, later ones become corroborating records; (b) reject the late sync and tell the user (they've gone home; nobody sees it); (c) accept blindly and duplicate. Recommendation: (a), with an idempotency key generated client-side at tick time so a retried sync can never double-write.
why_it_matters: Offline queues without idempotency keys reliably produce duplicates on flaky connections, and duplicates directly inflate or corrupt the scores.

topic: Phone battery dead / device unavailable
detail: The only tablet dies at 22:00 and the close-down list can't be ticked. Everything shows as missed. Options: (a) manager retro-completion the next morning with a reason (needs the override from the disputed-miss case); (b) a paper fallback that gets keyed in; (c) accept the misses. Recommendation: (a) — the retro-complete-with-reason path covers dead battery, dead wifi, broken tablet and forgotten ticks all at once. It's the single most important escape hatch in the system.
why_it_matters: Hardware fails weekly in a pub. Without a sanctioned recovery path, staff invent their own (ticking everything the next day from memory), which is worse and invisible.

topic: Data retention for checklist and score records
detail: Food-safety records (temperatures) have a genuine business/regulatory reason to be kept for a period, but personal performance scores don't need the same lifespan. Options: (a) one retention rule for everything (simple, over-retains personal data); (b) split — keep the food-safety reading and the fact the check happened for the food-safety retention period, but strip or anonymise the personal attribution and the derived score after a shorter window (e.g. 12–24 months); (c) keep everything forever. Recommendation: (b) — the fridge temperature is a business record; 'Jane scored 78% in March 2025' is personal data with no reason to outlive the employment relationship by much.
why_it_matters: Indefinitely retained per-person performance scores are exactly the sort of thing that turns into a subject-access-request or grievance problem, and there's no operational upside to keeping them.
decision_needed: How long do you want to keep (a) temperature/food-safety records and (b) per-person scores? Recommend keeping food-safety records per your EHO/HACCP practice and anonymising personal scores after 12 months.

topic: Leavers' scores and completion history
detail: A staff member leaves. Their name is on 400 completion records and last quarter's scores. Options: (a) hard-delete the person and orphan/anonymise every record — destroys the food-safety audit trail; (b) keep the records with the name (needed for the audit trail — 'who signed this fridge check' is the point of the record); (c) keep the food-safety attribution but remove them from live scoreboards/leaderboards and exclude from current-period reporting; (d) anonymise to 'former employee #12' after the retention window. Recommendation: (c) now, (d) later. Importantly, a leaver must drop out of the score denominator immediately so their absence doesn't drag the venue average.
why_it_matters: This project already has a documented erasure gap in recruitment (ai_runs PII not scrubbed). Building a second store of per-person data with no leaver story repeats the same Art. 17 problem.
decision_needed: On erasure request, do you anonymise the attribution on food-safety records or keep the name as a business/legal-obligation record? (Recommend: keep the name for the food-safety retention period under legal obligation, anonymise everything else.)

topic: Scoring denominator gaming
detail: If the score is 'completed / assigned', the way to a high score is to be assigned fewer tasks, or to remove tasks from the template, or to only work quiet shifts. Conversely a keen person who covers everyone's tasks may score worse if credit follows the assignee. Options: (a) score on rate but publish the denominator alongside it; (b) don't rank people against each other at all — show trend against their own history; (c) restrict template editing to super_admin so the denominator can't be quietly shrunk. Recommendation: (b)+(c) — trends beat leaderboards for this. Leaderboards in a small team of people who all know each other create friction out of proportion to the benefit.
why_it_matters: Any metric attached to a person will be optimised. Designing the metric so the easiest way to improve it is to do the job is the whole game.
decision_needed: Do you want a per-person leaderboard, or per-person trend-only with a venue-level leaderboard? (Recommend the latter.)

topic: Cron/instance-generation failure
detail: The nightly job that creates the day's instances fails silently (this codebase has form — the events Google Calendar reconciliation cron was never scheduled and its failures were swallowed). Result: no instances, so nothing shows as missed, so the report reads 100%. A perfect score is indistinguishable from a total outage. Options: (a) generation is idempotent on (checklist_id, business_date) and re-runnable, with a heartbeat/alert if a day has zero instances; (b) generate lazily on first view (no failure mode, but the 'missed' state needs a different anchor); (c) fire-and-forget. Recommendation: (a) — idempotent generation plus an explicit 'no instances generated for today' alarm.
why_it_matters: A silent generation failure makes the system report perfection while doing nothing, which is the most dangerous possible failure for a food-safety tool.

topic: Timing of the 'missed' transition
detail: Nothing marks an instance as missed unless something runs. If 'missed' is computed on read (due_at < now AND not completed), it's always correct and needs no cron; if it's a stored state set by a job, it can be stale or never set. Options: (a) derive missed at read time from due_at and completion; (b) a sweeper job that writes the missed state; (c) both, with the job only for triggering notifications. Recommendation: (a) for display and scoring, (b) only where you need an event to hang a notification off. Derived state can't rot.
why_it_matters: Stored status fields that depend on a cron are how you get a dashboard showing 'pending' at 3pm the next day, and they undermine confidence in every other number on the page.

topic: Route and page shell
detail: The FOH page lives at /table-bookings/foh, served by src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx:16 (server component). It renders a `<PageLayout>` (src/ds/composites/PageLayout.tsx:122) with title "Front of House Schedule", optional navItems (Back of House / Front of House / Reports), a backButton, and `headerActions`. Its only child is `<FohScheduleClient>` (page.tsx:125), a client component at foh/FohScheduleClient.tsx:38.
why_it_matters: Any new surface must decide whether it belongs in the server page (data pre-fetched, permission-gated) or inside the client component (interactive, realtime-aware).

topic: Where a new button would live
detail: Three realistic slots. (1) PageLayout `headerActions` — set in page.tsx:88-108; today that slot holds `<FohClockWidget>` + logo for the manager iPad, or a "Table Setup" LinkButton for settings-managers. (2) The in-page action row in FohHeader.tsx:225-297 — this is where "Food Order" (FohHeader.tsx:227-247), the Inside/Outside view toggle (250-267), "Walk-in" (271-282) and "Add booking" (283-294) already sit, all gated on `canEdit`. (3) Per-booking actions in components/FohBookingDetailModal.tsx:246-333 (a 2-col grid of action buttons wired through `onRunAction`).
why_it_matters: FohHeader's action row is the natural home for a page-level FOH action — it already has the canEdit gate, the kiosk-compact style variants, and the `lastInteractionAtMsRef` idle-tracking pattern a new button must respect.
decision_needed: Should a new button be a page-level action (FohHeader row) or a per-booking action (FohBookingDetailModal grid)?

topic: Chromeless / kiosk behaviour
detail: Two distinct mechanisms, often conflated. (a) FOH-only chromeless mode: `isFohOnlyUser(permissions)` (src/lib/foh/user-mode.ts:7) returns true when a user's permissions are non-empty and contain table_bookings:view AND nothing outside table_bookings. AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:31-34 uses it to pass `showSidebar={!fohOnlyMode}` and `fohMode` to AppShell (AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:171-172); AppShell.tsx:60-61,101 then drops the sidebar, mobile drawer and bottom nav. AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:92-100 also hard-redirects such users to /table-bookings/foh from any other path. (b) Manager iPad kiosk styling: page.tsx:65 `useManagerKioskStyle = user.email === 'manager@the-anchor.pub'` (constant at page.tsx:14), which only changes styling/density (`styleVariant: 'manager_kiosk'`, foh/types.ts:202) and swaps in the clock widget.
why_it_matters: The two modes are independent — the shared manager account is NOT foh-only (it has broad permissions), so it keeps the sidebar but gets kiosk styling. A new feature gated on the wrong one will appear/disappear unexpectedly.
decision_needed: Should a new button appear in both the default and manager_kiosk style variants, and for foh-only users as well as full-permission staff?

topic: Authentication — fully auth'd, not public
detail: The route sits inside the `(authenticated)` route group. src/middleware.ts:10-33 lists PUBLIC_PATH_PREFIXES — `/timeclock`, `/table-booking` (singular, the public site) and `/recruitment/book` are public, but `/table-bookings/foh` is NOT. src/app/(authenticated)/layout.tsx redirects to /auth/login when `supabase.auth.getUser()` returns no user, and foh/page.tsx:44-46 redirects to /unauthorized without `table_bookings:view`. The FOH API routes are guarded by `requireFohPermission` (src/lib/foh/api-auth.ts:9).
why_it_matters: There is no anonymous/kiosk-token path into FOH. Anything new on this page inherits a real Supabase session and a real user id — so audit logging and RBAC are available, unlike the genuinely-public /timeclock kiosk.

topic: Which login staff realistically use
detail: The shared `manager@the-anchor.pub` account, on the bar iPad. Evidence: page.tsx:14,65 special-cases exactly that email for kiosk styling; src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:21,55-66 has `canUseManagerFohKioskTimeclock()` which authorises timeclock actions purely because the caller is that email plus has table_bookings:view; and src/lib/events/refund-permissions.ts:6-13 explicitly blocks `manager@the-anchor.pub` from issuing refunds because it is "a communal account". A per-person foh-only login path also exists (isFohOnlyUser), and AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:103-120 resolves that user's `employee_id` by matching `employees.email_address` to the session email.
why_it_matters: In practice the session identity on the FOH iPad is a shared account, not a person. Any new action that needs to record WHO did it cannot rely on the session user — the audit trail would just say "manager".
decision_needed: If a new button needs personal attribution, do we accept 'manager' as the actor, or require an explicit person-picker/PIN like the timeclock flow?

topic: Existing "who is this" signals
detail: Three exist, none of them a device id. (1) Employee picker: FohClockWidget.tsx:129-140 renders a `<select>` of active employees ("Who's clocking in?") — a per-action choice, NOT persisted selected-user state; sessions are re-read from `getOpenSessions()` each load (page.tsx:80-85). (2) Timeclock PIN: `verifyClockIdentity()` (timeclock.ts:68-95) checks `timeclock_pin_hash`, falling back to the last four digits of the mobile number (`phoneLastFourMatchesPin`) — but timeclock.ts:90 lets an authenticated caller through with NO PIN, which is exactly how the FOH kiosk widget works. (3) Personal FOH band: AppShell.tsx:86-87 renders `<FohClockBand employeeId={fohEmployeeId}>` (src/ds/shell/FohClockBand.tsx:14) only for foh-only users whose email maps to an employee row. No device id, no localStorage identity, no cookie-scoped terminal in src/lib/foh/ or src/components/foh/.
why_it_matters: The clock widget is the closest thing to a 'who is this' signal, but it is transient and PIN-less on the kiosk. Currently anyone at the iPad can clock any employee in or out with no verification. A new feature needing an actor must either add its own picker or reuse the open-sessions list as an implicit 'who is on shift' hint.
decision_needed: Should a new who-is-this signal reuse the open timeclock sessions (who's clocked in right now) as the candidate list, rather than introducing a separate identity mechanism?

topic: Constraints a new button must respect
detail: The page auto-returns to today after 5 minutes of inactivity (FohScheduleClient.tsx:179-192), suppressed while `hasActiveFohWork` is true (FohScheduleClient.tsx:173-177) — any new modal/in-flight state must be added to that boolean or it will be interrupted mid-use. Interaction handlers must bump `lastInteractionAtMsRef` (pattern at FohHeader.tsx:182). Realtime reloads are paused during drags (FohScheduleClient.tsx:109). Style must branch on `isManagerKioskStyle` for the compact iPad density (FohHeader.tsx:56, and e.g. 232-235).
why_it_matters: Skipping the `hasActiveFohWork` registration is a silent bug — the screen will jump back to today while a member of staff is halfway through the new flow.

topic: End-to-end clock-in/clock-out flow
detail: Three UI surfaces all funnel into two server actions in `/Users/peterpitcher/Cursor/OJ-AnchorManagementTools/src/app/actions/timeclock.ts`: `clockIn(employeeId, pin?)` at line 175 and `clockOut(employeeId, pin?)` at line 250. Both use the service-role admin client (line 18: `const createClient = () => createAdminClient()`) precisely because the kiosk has no Supabase auth session. Sequence for clock-in: load employee (182), gate on status in ['Active','Started Separation'] (186), verify identity (188), reject if an open session exists (196-206), insert `timeclock_sessions` row with `clock_in_at` + London `work_date` (211-219), then `linkSessionToShift()` (229, defined 346) matches a `rota_shifts` row on same date within ±2h of shift start, else flags `is_unscheduled`. Clock-out: same employee load + identity check (255-268), find the open session (270-276), UPDATE `clock_out_at` (284-290). Both invalidate payroll approvals for that date and revalidate `/timeclock` + `/rota/timeclock`.
why_it_matters: Any checklist warning has exactly one server-side chokepoint (`clockOut`) but three separate client surfaces — you must decide whether the warning is enforced server-side, rendered client-side, or both.
decision_needed: Should the checklist warning be a server-side gate inside `clockOut()` (covers all surfaces, including future ones) or a client-side pre-flight check per UI (better UX, but three implementations)?

topic: Yes — it is a public kiosk with a PIN
detail: `/timeclock` is publicly routable: `src/middleware.ts:29` lists `'/timeclock'` in `PUBLIC_PATH_PREFIXES`, and `src/app/(timeclock)/layout.tsx` is a full-screen kiosk shell whose own comment says "No authentication required — accessible on the till iPad." The page (`src/app/(timeclock)/timeclock/page.tsx:9-20`) uses `createAdminClient()` directly to list every Active / Started Separation employee with no auth session at all.
why_it_matters: Anything you surface at clock-out on the kiosk is visible to anyone standing at the till, and any data you fetch for it is fetched with the service-role key on an unauthenticated page. Checklist contents must be safe to render publicly.
decision_needed: Is it acceptable for outstanding-checklist text to be visible on an unauthenticated public kiosk screen, or should the kiosk show only a generic count and the detail live behind auth?

topic: What identifies the employee — employee_id UUID + 4-digit PIN, NOT auth
detail: The kiosk renders one button per employee carrying the raw `employee_id` UUID (`TimeclockClient.tsx:139-153`); tapping opens the PIN modal (`handleClock`, line 56-64, with a UUID format guard at 57). Identity is proven by `verifyClockIdentity()` in `src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:68-95`: if a PIN is supplied it checks `employees.timeclock_pin_hash` via scrypt (`src/lib/timeclock/pin.ts:24-38`); **if no hash is stored it falls back to `phoneLastFourMatchesPin()` — the last 4 digits of `mobile_number` or `phone_number`** (`timeclock.ts:81`, `pin.ts:40-50`). If NO pin is supplied it falls through to `canUseAuthenticatedTimeclock()` (line 90) requiring `timeclock:clock` / `timeclock:edit`, or a hardcoded manager-iPad fallback: `MANAGER_IPAD_EMAIL = 'manager@the-anchor.pub'` (line 21, checked 55-66). PINs are set by a manager on the employee form (`EmployeeForm.tsx:119`, hashed at `employeeActions.ts:404`).
why_it_matters: I checked live prod: of 11 active employees, **0 have a `timeclock_pin_hash` set** — all 11 have a mobile number. So in practice every kiosk clock-in/out today authenticates on the last 4 digits of the employee's phone number, which colleagues plausibly know. The kiosk also lists every employee's name publicly, so the only secret is 4 digits of a semi-known phone number.
decision_needed: Do you want real PINs issued to the 11 active employees (killing the phone-last-4 fallback), given a checklist warning at clock-out makes the identity check more consequential?

topic: Exact clock-OUT injection points (file:line)
detail: Server-side (single chokepoint, all surfaces): `src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:250` `clockOut()` — the DB write itself is at lines 284-290. Client surfaces calling it: (1) **Public kiosk** — `src/app/(timeclock)/timeclock/_components/TimeclockClient.tsx:80`, inside `submitPin()`; (2) **FOH table-bookings widget** — `src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/FohClockWidget.tsx:62`, inside `handleClockOut()`; (3) **FOH chromeless shell band** — `src/ds/shell/FohClockBand.tsx:56`, inside `handleClockOut()`. Two non-interactive paths also write `clock_out_at` and bypass every UI: the auto-close cron at `src/app/api/cron/rota-auto-close/route.ts:88-99`, and manager corrections via `updateTimeclockSession()` at `src/app/actions/timeclock.ts:646` (write at 704-716).
why_it_matters: A warning injected only in `TimeclockClient.tsx` leaves three other ways to clock out un-warned. The cron auto-closes any session still open at ~05:00 — those employees will never see a warning at all.
decision_needed: Should the auto-close cron (route.ts:88) also flag/record outstanding checklist items, since those staff never pass through a clock-out UI?

topic: Modal flow vs single button — it varies by surface
detail: **Kiosk**: two-step and already modal. Tap employee card → PIN modal (`TimeclockClient.tsx:162-209`) with a `Cancel` (194) and `Confirm` (199-205) button; the modal header already says "Clock out" vs "Clock in" (line 173). **FOH widget**: has a genuine, purpose-built confirmation — `ConfirmDialog` at `FohClockWidget.tsx:147-160`, message "Ready to clock out, {name}? You clocked in at {time}.", confirm label "Yes, clock out", cancel "Not yet". **FohClockBand** (`src/ds/shell/FohClockBand.tsx:78-87`): a single `Clock Out` button, **no confirmation, no PIN** — one click writes the clock-out. It's live, rendered from `AppShell.tsx:87` when `fohMode && fohEmployeeId` (fed from `AuthenticatedLayout.tsx:30`).
why_it_matters: Two of the three surfaces already have a modal you can extend cheaply. `FohClockBand` has no dialog at all, so a warning there needs a new modal built from scratch — or it silently stays un-warned.
decision_needed: For the kiosk, do you want the warning inside the existing PIN modal (fewer taps) or as a second modal after PIN success (harder to ignore)? I'd recommend a second step after PIN success — the PIN modal is muscle-memory and a warning there will be tapped straight through.

topic: Existing confirmation step
detail: Only `FohClockWidget.tsx:147` is a true confirmation (`ConfirmDialog` from `@/ds`, variant="primary"). The kiosk's PIN modal is a de facto confirm (Cancel/Confirm at `TimeclockClient.tsx:191-205`) but its purpose is identity, not intent. `FohClockBand` has none. There is no existing 'are you sure / outstanding items' pattern anywhere in the clock-out path.
why_it_matters: There's a reusable `ConfirmDialog` in the design system already proven on this exact flow — the warning can reuse it on the FOH widget and FohClockBand rather than inventing a new component.

topic: Employee ↔ auth user link
detail: `employees.auth_user_id UUID REFERENCES auth.users(id) ON DELETE SET NULL`, added in `supabase/migrations/20260227000001_employee_invite_onboarding.sql:39`, with a unique partial index (lines 43-44, re-asserted in `20260308120000_employees_constraints_and_indexes.sql:39-41`). It's populated by the invite flow's `link_employee_invite_account(p_token, p_auth_user_id)` RPC (`supabase/migrations/20260628000000_employee_invite_flow_hardening.sql:74`, sets the column at 127/137), called from `src/app/actions/employeeInvite.ts:548`. Reverse lookups are `.eq('auth_user_id', user.id)` — e.g. `src/app/(staff-portal)/portal/shifts/page.tsx:476`, `src/app/actions/rota.ts:442` and `:1909`, `src/app/actions/leave.ts:92`. Live prod: all 11 active employees have `auth_user_id` populated. Crucially, **the timeclock path never touches `auth_user_id`** — it keys purely on `employee_id` + PIN.
why_it_matters: If checklist items are owned by an auth user (e.g. assigned via the portal), you need the `employees.auth_user_id` hop to join them to the kiosk's `employee_id`. Conveniently, every active employee already has both, so the join is total today — but the column is nullable and new starters won't have it until they accept an invite.
decision_needed: Should checklist items key off `employee_id` (works on the kiosk with zero joins, and for staff who never accept an invite) rather than `auth_user_id`? I'd recommend `employee_id`.

topic: Employee email addresses
detail: `employees.email_address` is `text NOT NULL` — confirmed against live prod schema, and in the table definition at `supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:2277`, with a format CHECK (`chk_employee_email_format`) and a 255-char length cap. TypeScript type at `src/types/database.ts:540`. Live prod: all 11 active/separating employees have an email address on file. There is no separate work/personal email column — one column only. (Note `src/types/database.generated.ts` is stale per prior findings; trust the live schema.)
why_it_matters: If the warning needs an email escalation (e.g. 'you clocked out with 3 items outstanding'), the address is guaranteed present and non-null for every employee — no fallback path needed. The app already has `sendEmail()` in `src/lib/email/emailService.ts` (Graph/Resend dual transport).
decision_needed: Do you want an email follow-up when someone clocks out with outstanding items, or is the on-screen warning enough? I'd recommend on-screen only for now — email adds a whole notification/audit surface for what may be a soft nudge.

topic: Event checklist: task model is a hardcoded TS constant, not data
detail: Tasks are defined in a static array `EVENT_CHECKLIST_DEFINITIONS` at src/lib/event-checklist.ts:30-39 — 8 items, each `{ key, label, offsetDays, channel, required, order }` (e.g. `write_event_brief` at offsetDays -28, `send_whatsapp_reminder` at 0). There is no task-definition table. Due dates are computed, never stored: `addDays(event.date, offsetDays)` at src/lib/event-checklist.ts:44-48, and status is derived by comparing the computed due date to today (src/lib/event-checklist.ts:68-77 → 'completed' | 'overdue' | 'due_today' | 'upcoming'). Adding, renaming or reordering a task is a code deploy, not a config change.
why_it_matters: If the new checklists feature needs user-editable tasks, recurring schedules, or per-instance due dates, this model gives you nothing to reuse — the concept of a task doesn't exist in the database at all. If you only need a fixed, developer-owned list bolted to a parent record, the pattern is small and works.
decision_needed: Should checklist task definitions be database rows (user-editable) or hardcoded constants like the event checklist?

topic: Event checklist: the only DB table is a thin completion ledger with no attribution
detail: Single table `event_checklist_statuses`, DDL at supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:8242-8249: `id, event_id (FK→events, ON DELETE CASCADE), task_key TEXT, completed_at TIMESTAMPTZ, created_at, updated_at`, `UNIQUE (event_id, task_key)`. I confirmed the live prod schema matches — there is NO `completed_by` column. Attribution exists only as an audit_logs side-effect written by the server action (src/app/actions/event-checklist.ts:53-66, resource_id `${eventId}:${taskKey}`), so the UI can never show who ticked a box — EventChecklistCard.tsx:247 only prints 'Completed <date>'. Rows marked incomplete are hard-deleted (src/services/event-checklist.ts:80-91), so un-ticking destroys the history too.
why_it_matters: 'Who did this and when' is usually the whole point of a shared checklist. The existing table cannot answer it, and the delete-on-untick behaviour means you can't reconstruct it either. Any new checklists feature needs `completed_by` from day one — retrofitting it onto the event table later means backfilling from audit_logs.
decision_needed: Does the new checklists feature need per-task attribution (completed_by) and assignment (assigned_to) in the table itself?

topic: Event checklist has serious, live code↔DB drift — treat as a warning, not a template
detail: Three sources disagree about what the tasks are. Code defines 8 keys (src/lib/event-checklist.ts:30-39). The nightly pg_cron auto-close function hardcodes a DIFFERENT list of 12 keys (supabase/migrations/20260228000004_auto_close_past_event_tasks.sql:15-28) — it includes `update_event_details`, `publish_event_page`, `create_short_link`, `design_bar_strut_cards`, `design_poster` which no longer exist in code, and OMITS `setup_paid_advertising` which does. Prod data has 23 distinct task_keys ever written (incl. `create_nextdoor_event`, `list_on_skiddle`, `setup_opentable_experience`, `generate_qr_code`). Net effect: `setup_paid_advertising` is never auto-closed on past events, and the code silently ignores 15 orphaned keys because `EVENT_CHECKLIST_TASK_KEYS.has(...)` filters them out (src/app/actions/event-checklist.ts:109).
why_it_matters: This is exactly the failure mode a hardcoded-list design invites: the task list has churned repeatedly and each churn left the SQL, the code and the data further apart, with no error raised. It is direct evidence for putting task definitions in the database for the new feature.
decision_needed: Should the drift between the auto-close pg_cron function and EVENT_CHECKLIST_DEFINITIONS be fixed as part of this work, or parked as separate tech debt?

topic: Event checklist bypasses RLS entirely — its RLS policies are decorative
detail: Every read and write uses `createAdminClient()` (service role, bypasses RLS): src/services/event-checklist.ts:18, 60, 97 and src/app/actions/event-checklist.ts:91. Meanwhile the migration defines four careful RLS policies gated on `user_has_permission(auth.uid(), 'events', 'view'|'manage')` (squashed.sql:8270-8299) that are never exercised. Real enforcement is the TS permission check in the server action only: `checkUserPermission('events','view')` for reads (event-checklist.ts:23, 82, 128) and `checkUserPermission('events','manage')` for the toggle (event-checklist.ts:42). Note also that the SQL `user_has_permission` function (squashed.sql:1629-1643) has NO super_admin short-circuit, whereas the TS PermissionService DOES (src/services/permission.ts:185-189) — so the two paths would disagree if RLS were ever relied on.
why_it_matters: Defence-in-depth is absent: a bug that skips the action-layer check has nothing behind it. If you copy this pattern for checklists you inherit the same single point of failure. Use the cookie-based client for reads so RLS is a real second gate.
decision_needed: Should the new checklists feature enforce via RLS + the anon/cookie client (defence in depth), or copy the existing admin-client + action-check-only pattern?

topic: Event checklist: layering is sound and worth copying even if the model isn't
detail: Clean three-layer split you should mirror: pure functions in src/lib/event-checklist.ts (no DB, testable — `buildEventChecklist`, `getOutstandingTodos`), DB access in src/services/event-checklist.ts (EventChecklistService), and permission + audit + revalidate in src/app/actions/event-checklist.ts. The action returns the standard `{ success, error }` envelope and calls revalidatePath('/events') and `/events/${eventId}` (event-checklist.ts:68-69). Toggle is a single upsert on the `event_id,task_key` conflict target (src/services/event-checklist.ts:63-72) — idempotent, no read-modify-write race. Dead code to not copy: `calculateChecklistProgress` (src/lib/event-checklist.ts:110-120) is defined and never used, and `getEventChecklistProgress` (src/app/actions/event-checklist.ts:81) is unexported and uncalled — both invisible because `@typescript-eslint/no-unused-vars` is off.
why_it_matters: The structure is reusable even though the schema isn't. Lifting the lib/service/action split and the upsert-on-conflict toggle saves design time and matches what a reviewer expects in this codebase.

topic: Event checklist surfaces: card, cross-event todo page, and a daily email cron
detail: Three consumers. (1) EventChecklistCard (src/components/features/events/EventChecklistCard.tsx) — rendered twice from src/app/(authenticated)/events/[id]/EventDetailClient.tsx:639 and :648; client component, fetches via server action on mount, optimistic tick with rollback (lines 53-85), splits Outstanding/Completed, Reopen button. (2) Cross-event page /events/todo (src/app/(authenticated)/events/todo/page.tsx) — gated on `checkUserPermission('events','view')` → redirect('/unauthorized'), renders TodoClient with only overdue + due-today items (src/services/event-checklist.ts:139). (3) Daily 08:00 UTC cron `/api/cron/event-checklist-reminders` (vercel.json:165, schedule '0 8 * * *') — emails a single hardcoded recipient (`EVENT_CHECKLIST_EMAIL_RECIPIENT` defaulting to peter@orangejelly.co.uk, route.ts:14), with proper idempotency claim/release around the send.
why_it_matters: There is a working reference for all three shapes you'll likely need: per-record card, cross-record roll-up, and a nag email. The email idempotency pattern in particular (claimIdempotencyKey → send → persistIdempotencyResponse, with release on failure) is the house standard and should be copied verbatim.

topic: /events/todo is orphaned from navigation — reachable only via the cron email
detail: Grep across src/ finds exactly zero links to `/events/todo` outside the cron email body (src/app/api/cron/event-checklist-reminders/route.ts:149 and :185). It is not in NAV_GROUPS (src/ds/shell/SidebarNav.tsx:28-79) and nothing on the Events pages links to it. Staff can only reach the cross-event checklist roll-up by clicking the daily email — which goes to one person.
why_it_matters: The roll-up view already exists and is effectively invisible. If the new checklists feature wants a cross-record view, either surface this one properly or expect the new one to be similarly stranded unless a nav entry is planned in.
decision_needed: Should the existing /events/todo page get a nav entry (or an Events-page link) as part of this work, or be folded into the new checklists section?

topic: Employee onboarding checklist: columns-as-tasks — a genuine dead end
detail: Tasks are boolean COLUMNS on `employee_onboarding_checklist`, one row per employee (upsert on `employee_id`, src/services/employees.ts:933-935). Each task is a `<field>` boolean + a paired `<field>_date`, mapped in ONBOARDING_FIELD_CONFIG (src/services/employees.ts:284-290): private_whatsapp_added, team_whatsapp_added, till_system_setup, training_flow_setup, employee_agreement_accepted. UI: src/components/features/employees/OnboardingChecklistTab.tsx (live — imported by src/app/(authenticated)/employees/[employee_id]/page.tsx:21,244). No due dates at all — no offsets, no overdue/due-today concept, no reminders. Attribution: none (no completed_by column in prod). Adding a task = a migration adding two columns + a code change.
why_it_matters: This is strictly weaker than the event checklist and must not be the template: adding a task requires a schema migration, so a user-editable checklist is impossible by construction. It also can't express due dates, which is likely a core requirement.

topic: Employee onboarding: undocumented DDL and two tasks hidden from the UI
detail: The `employee_onboarding_checklist` table has NO CREATE TABLE anywhere in supabase/migrations/ or supabase/migrations-archive/ — it exists in prod only (migrations merely INSERT into it, e.g. 20260227000001_employee_invite_onboarding.sql:110). Live prod has 7 boolean task columns but the TS config exposes only 5: `wheniwork_invite_sent` (+ wheniwork_invite_date) and `employment_agreement_drafted` (+ employment_agreement_date) exist in the DB and are completely absent from ONBOARDING_CHECKLIST_FIELDS (src/services/employees.ts:277-282), so they are unreachable and uncounted in the progress percentage. Also note the hardcoded 'Important Notes' block (OnboardingChecklistTab.tsx:176-184) hardcodes policy copy in the component.
why_it_matters: Same drift disease as the event checklist, plus the schema isn't reproducible from the repo — a fresh environment cannot be built from migrations. If this feature migrates onto a new checklists model, those two orphan columns need a decision (backfill or drop), and dropping requires the mandatory function audit per the Supabase rules.
decision_needed: Are `wheniwork_invite_sent` and `employment_agreement_drafted` dead (drop) or genuinely wanted (expose in the UI)?

topic: RBAC: 'checklists' is free — no module or action collision
detail: ModuleName union at src/types/rbac.ts:31-63 has 32 entries (dashboard, events, performers, customers, employees, messages, sms_health, settings, reports, users, roles, private_bookings, table_bookings, invoices, oj_projects, receipts, loyalty, quotes, parking, short_links, feedback, menu_management, cashing_up, rota, leave, timeclock, payroll, recruitment, mileage, expenses, mgd). ActionType union at src/types/rbac.ts:65-108 has 44 entries; view/create/edit/delete/manage/export/publish/request/assign-style actions all present ('assign' is NOT — you'd add it). Live prod `permissions` has 35 module_names; 'checklists' is not among them. Note the union and the DB have drifted: prod has audit_logs, bookings, employee_history, invoicing (a duplicate of invoices) that the TS union lacks. The `permissions` table (squashed.sql:2804-2809) is plain text columns with NO check constraint, so a new module is pure INSERT — no enum migration needed.
why_it_matters: Adding a 'checklists' module is unblocked and cheap: one union edit plus one seeding migration. The absence of a DB constraint means a typo in module_name fails silently as a permanent permission-denied, so the TS union is the only guard — keep them in sync.
decision_needed: New top-level 'checklists' module, or reuse existing per-domain modules (events:manage, employees:edit) to gate checklists within each section?

topic: RBAC: how checkUserPermission actually resolves, and the super_admin short-circuit
detail: `checkUserPermission(module, action, userId?)` (src/app/actions/rbac.ts:63-79) resolves the user via `supabase.auth.getUser()` when userId is omitted, then delegates to `PermissionService.checkUserPermission` (src/services/permission.ts:180-193). That function first checks a 60s-cached super_admin role lookup (`get_user_roles` RPC, permission.ts:162-175, cache tag `permissions-${userId}`) and returns true unconditionally for super_admin — even with zero permission rows. Otherwise it matches against `get_user_permissions` RPC results, also cached 60s (permission.ts:145-158). Both use the admin client. Critically, the SQL-side `user_has_permission` (squashed.sql:1629-1643) has NO super_admin short-circuit — it is a plain join through user_roles→role_permissions→permissions.
why_it_matters: Two consequences for planning. First, the 60s cache means a newly seeded permission won't take effect instantly after a role change unless the tag is busted. Second, super_admin passing in TS but failing in SQL means any RLS policy you write for checklists MUST have explicit super_admin permission rows seeded — you cannot rely on the short-circuit at the DB layer.

topic: RBAC: permission seeding follows a copy-paste migration template
detail: Permissions are seeded as DATA by per-feature migrations, not in the squash. Best template is supabase/migrations/20260302000001_short_links_rbac_permissions.sql: an idempotent DO block that (a) `IF NOT EXISTS (SELECT 1 FROM permissions WHERE module_name=... AND action=...)` then INSERTs each permission, (b) `SELECT id INTO super_admin_role_id FROM roles WHERE name='super_admin'`, same for manager, (c) INSERT INTO role_permissions SELECT role_id, p.id FROM permissions p WHERE p.module_name=... AND NOT EXISTS (...). Other examples: 20260405130000_mileage_expenses_mgd.sql (added three new modules at once — closest analogue to adding 'checklists'), 20260703090000_feedback_rbac_permissions.sql, 20260120132000_add_oj_projects_permissions.sql. Roles are super_admin, manager, staff. I confirmed events:manage exists in prod, so the current checklist toggle gate is real.
why_it_matters: Adding a 'checklists' module is a well-trodden path — copy the short_links migration, swap the module name. The idempotent NOT EXISTS guards matter because these migrations get re-run against environments in unknown states.
decision_needed: Which roles get checklists:manage — super_admin + manager only (matching short_links), or should staff get view/complete?

topic: Navigation: adding a permission-gated nav item is a one-line change
detail: NAV_GROUPS is a flat exported array of 5 unlabelled groups at src/ds/shell/SidebarNav.tsx:28-79. Each NavItem is `{ id, label, icon (IconName from @/ds/icons), href, badge?, permission?: { module: ModuleName, action: ActionType } }` (interface at SidebarNav.tsx:11-21). Gating is automatic: `filterNavGroupsForPermissions` (SidebarNav.tsx:81-94) drops any item whose permission fails and drops groups left empty; items with no `permission` key (e.g. 'profile', line 76) are always shown. Active state is prefix-based — `pathname === href || pathname.startsWith(href + '/')` (SidebarNav.tsx:131-134). Consumed by both desktop Sidebar and MobileChrome, so one edit covers both. Optional badge: add the id to the `countById` map in `navCount` (SidebarNav.tsx:109-118) and wire an OutstandingCounts field — a live count of 0 correctly wins over a static badge (comment at :120-122).
why_it_matters: Zero friction — a checklists entry is one object in the array plus optionally one line in countById. The Events group (SidebarNav.tsx:32-37) is the natural home if checklists are event-scoped; a new item in the Employees group (:51-54) if people-scoped.
decision_needed: Where should a checklists nav item live — its own entry in the first group, or nested under Events/Employees? And does it want an outstanding-count badge?

topic: Verdict: reuse the layering, not the schema
detail: The event checklist is a working, deployed, actively-used system (112 events with completion rows in prod) — not a dead end, but its schema is a local optimum for exactly one use case: a fixed developer-owned list hung off a parent record, with no attribution and no assignment. The onboarding checklist IS effectively a dead end (columns-as-tasks, no due dates, undocumented DDL, hidden orphan columns). Recommendation: build a proper `checklist_templates` / `checklist_template_tasks` / `checklist_instances` / `checklist_task_statuses` model with `completed_by`, `assigned_to` and stored due dates; copy the lib/service/action layering from src/lib|services|app/actions/event-checklist.ts, the upsert-on-conflict toggle, and the cron idempotency pattern; enforce via RLS with the cookie client rather than the admin client; and seed a 'checklists' module using the short_links migration template.
why_it_matters: The two existing systems together demonstrate the exact cost of hardcoded task lists — 23 orphaned task keys in prod data and a pg_cron function that has silently disagreed with the code since February. Building on either would repeat that.
decision_needed: Do we migrate the two existing checklists onto the new model (one-off data migration), or leave them alone and let the new feature stand beside them?

topic: Email — sendEmail signature
detail: `sendEmail(options: EmailOptions): Promise<{ success: boolean; error?: string; messageId?: string }>` at src/lib/email/emailService.ts:111. It takes a single options object, NOT positional args (CLAUDE.md's `sendEmail(to, subject, html, cc?, attachments?)` is stale). EmailOptions (src/lib/email/emailService.ts:8-30): to, subject, html?, text?, cc?, bcc?, attachments?, provider?, from?, graphSender?, replyTo?, commType?, customerId?, requireLog?, metadata?, plus entity FK fields (tableBookingId, eventBookingId, privateBookingId, parkingBookingId, invoiceId, quoteId). Dual transport picked by EMAIL_PROVIDER, falling back to Resend when RESEND_API_KEY + EMAIL_FROM_ADDRESS are set (src/lib/email/emailService.ts:42-49). Suppression list is checked first and returns `{success:false, error:'Recipient email address is suppressed'}` (emailService.ts:112-126).
why_it_matters: Any new automated email must call the object-form API and can only rely on the documented return shape; a positional call will not compile.

topic: Email — how templates are done
detail: No templating engine. Templates are plain exported functions returning HTML strings, grouped per domain: src/lib/rota/email-templates.ts (buildStaffRotaEmailHtml:77, buildRotaChangeEmailHtml:140, buildManagerAlertEmailHtml:266, buildHolidaySubmittedEmailHtml:294, buildHolidayDecisionEmailHtml:319, buildShiftRejectedManagerEmailHtml:349, buildOpenShiftRequestManagerEmailHtml:376, buildShiftAutoAcceptWarningEmailHtml:443, buildEarningsAlertEmailHtml:486, buildPayrollEmailHtml:533). Each wraps content in `<div style="font-family:sans-serif;max-width:600px;margin:0 auto">` with inline styles. Other template modules: src/lib/email/employee-invite-emails.ts, private-booking-emails.ts, event-ticket-emails.ts, calendar-invite.ts.
why_it_matters: A new employee email should add a `build...EmailHtml()` function to src/lib/rota/email-templates.ts and follow the same inline-style, 600px-max-width pattern — no new dependency needed.

topic: Email — cited example of an automated email TO AN EMPLOYEE
detail: BEST EXAMPLE: src/lib/rota/send-rota-emails.ts:36 `sendRotaWeekEmails(weekId, weekStart)` — sends each employee their published shifts. It (a) loads employees via admin client filtering `.in('status', ['Active','Started Separation'])` and reads `email_address` (send-rota-emails.ts:44-47), (b) filters to those with an email address (line 76-78), (c) calls sendEmail at line 105-109 with `{to, subject, html: buildStaffRotaEmailHtml(...)}`, (d) inserts a row into `rota_email_log` recording sent/failed (line 111-119), (e) uses Promise.allSettled and returns `{sent, errors}`. It is invoked by the cron at src/app/api/cron/rota-staff-email/route.ts:49. Second example (email to a MANAGER, not staff): src/app/api/cron/rota-manager-alert/route.ts:50-76 — resolves the recipient from `system_settings` key `rota_manager_email` falling back to env ROTA_MANAGER_EMAIL, then sendEmail + rota_email_log insert.
why_it_matters: This is the exact template to copy for any new automated employee email: admin client, employee status filter, email_address field, sendEmail, rota_email_log audit row.

topic: Email — audit log table for rota emails
detail: `public.rota_email_log` defined at supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:272. Columns: email_type (CHECK-constrained), entity_type, entity_id, to_addresses TEXT[], cc_addresses, subject, status ('sent'|'failed'), error_message, message_id, sent_by (NULL = automated), sent_at. The email_type CHECK was replaced at supabase/migrations/20260704000000_portal_shift_acceptance.sql:268-281 and now allows exactly: staff_rota, staff_rota_change, manager_alert, holiday_submitted, holiday_decision, holiday_manager_notify, payroll_export, shift_rejected, shift_auto_accept_warning, open_shift_requested.
why_it_matters: A new email type CANNOT be logged to rota_email_log without a migration extending the CHECK constraint — inserts will fail at runtime otherwise. This is a required migration if the new email is rota-related.
decision_needed: Should a new automated email log to rota_email_log (needs a CHECK-extending migration) or rely only on emailService's own recordEmailMessage logging?

topic: Cron — auth pattern
detail: Every route under src/app/api/cron/* uses `authorizeCronRequest(request)` from src/lib/cron-auth.ts:22 and returns 401 on failure. Canonical usage: src/app/api/cron/rota-staff-email/route.ts:19-22. The helper compares the `authorization` header against `Bearer ${CRON_SECRET}` (or bare CRON_SECRET) using a sha256 + timingSafeEqual constant-time compare (cron-auth.ts:9-20, 41-44). The `x-vercel-cron: 1` header is explicitly NOT trusted for auth — it is diagnostics only (cron-auth.ts:46-51). If CRON_SECRET is unset: authorized in non-production, refused in production (cron-auth.ts:27-37).
why_it_matters: Any new cron route must call authorizeCronRequest first; skipping it or trusting x-vercel-cron would leave the endpoint publicly callable.

topic: Cron — schedule patterns in use and frequent crons
detail: vercel.json defines 40 crons. Frequent crons ALREADY EXIST: `* * * * *` (every minute — /api/jobs/process?process=true&batch=30); `*/5 * * * *` (event-booking-holds, event-waitlist-offers); `*/15 * * * *` (9 routes: event-paypal-reconciliation, event-payment-reminders, event-guest-engagement, pub-ops-event-calendar-sync, pub-ops-calendar-note-sync, parking-notifications, reconcile-sms, paypal-deposit-reconciliation); `*/30 * * * *` (recruitment-calendar-retry, recruitment-ai-sweep); hourly `0 * * * *` (private-bookings-weekly-summary, table-booking-deposit-timeout, recruitment-reminders). Daily patterns: `0 9 * * *`, `30 8 * * *`, `0 8 * * *`, `0 7 * * *`, `0 6 * * *`, `15 6 * * *`, `0 5 * * *`, `30 7 * * *`, `0 2 * * *`, `0 3 * * *`, `0 1 * * *`, `5 1 * * *`. Weekly: `0 18 * * 0`, `0 21 * * 0`, `30 3 * * 0`, `0 0 * * 1`. Monthly: `0 0 1 * *`.
why_it_matters: There is precedent and headroom for a 15-minute or hourly cron; adding one is a vercel.json entry plus a route, no plan/limit concern raised by existing usage.

topic: Cron — crons run in UTC, London handled in code
detail: Vercel cron schedules are UTC. Routes that must fire at a London wall-clock time re-check the local time and bail out if wrong: src/app/api/cron/rota-staff-email/route.ts:24-29 (`if (nowLocal.getDay() !== 0) return skipped`), and src/app/api/cron/rota-auto-close/route.ts:22-33 which accepts a 04:00–06:00 local window explicitly to absorb DST drift. TIMEZONE = 'Europe/London' constant + date-fns-tz toZonedTime/formatInTimeZone throughout.
why_it_matters: Any new time-of-day-sensitive cron must apply the same guard, or it will fire an hour early/late for half the year (BST).

topic: Rota — which table tells us who is on shift
detail: TWO tables, and the distinction matters. `public.rota_shifts` (supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:50) is the LIVE MANAGER DRAFT. `public.rota_published_shifts` (supabase/migrations/20260503000002_published_shifts_snapshot.sql:5) is a SNAPSHOT of the last-published version, keyed by the same UUID as rota_shifts.id; its header comment states 'Staff portal reads from here; managers edit rota_shifts (live draft)'. Both carry: week_id, employee_id, shift_date DATE, start_time TIME, end_time TIME, unpaid_break_minutes, department, status, notes, is_overnight, is_open_shift, name. Publish state lives on `rota_weeks` (rota_system.sql:11, status CHECK IN ('draft','published'), plus has_unpublished_changes).
why_it_matters: For anything staff-facing or reflecting what people were actually told to work, read rota_published_shifts, not rota_shifts — otherwise unpublished draft edits leak.
decision_needed: Should a 'who was on the closing shift' feature read the published snapshot (what staff were told) or the live draft (what managers last edited)? Recommendation: rota_published_shifts, matching sendRotaWeekEmails.

topic: Rota — statuses that exist
detail: rota_shifts.status: `TEXT NOT NULL DEFAULT 'scheduled' CHECK (status IN ('scheduled','sick','cancelled'))` — supabase/migrations/20260228100000_rota_system.sql:60. No later migration alters this CHECK. 'sick' is surfaced in the UI as "Couldn't Work" (src/app/(authenticated)/rota/ShiftDetailModal.tsx:80, and sick_reason → "Couldn't Work reason" at :99, `isCouldntWork = shift.status === 'sick'` at :204) — so absences are rota_shifts rows with status='sick', NOT leave_requests. rota_published_shifts.status is a plain TEXT DEFAULT 'scheduled' with NO check constraint (published_shifts_snapshot.sql:14). Related: rota_weeks.status ('draft','published'), leave_requests.status ('pending','approved','declined') at rota_system.sql:93.
why_it_matters: Any 'who worked' query must filter `.eq('status','scheduled')` or it will count sick and cancelled shifts as worked — every existing query does this (e.g. send-rota-emails.ts:53, dashboard-data.ts:1560).

topic: Rota — open shifts and the employee_id nullability trap
detail: supabase/migrations/20260228100002_open_shifts.sql:4-16 made employee_id NULLABLE and added `is_open_shift BOOLEAN NOT NULL DEFAULT FALSE`, with CHECK `rota_shifts_open_shift_check`: (is_open_shift=TRUE AND employee_id IS NULL) OR (is_open_shift=FALSE AND employee_id IS NOT NULL). The canonical filter for 'a real person is on this shift' is therefore three predicates: `.eq('status','scheduled').eq('is_open_shift', false).not('employee_id','is',null)` — see src/app/(authenticated)/dashboard/dashboard-data.ts:1557-1563 and src/lib/rota/send-rota-emails.ts:51-56.
why_it_matters: Omitting the is_open_shift filter yields unassigned shifts with a null employee_id, which will crash or silently drop rows in any 'who was working' lookup.

topic: Rota — CAN we determine 'who was working the closing shift on date X'? YES
detail: Yes, two independent ways, and the naming convention makes it reliable. (1) BY NAME: live prod `rota_shift_templates` (verified by read-only query against project tfcasgxopxegwrabvwat) are named exactly '<Day> Open' / '<Day> Close' — active bar closers are 'Monday'(16:00-22:00), 'Wednesday'(16:00-22:00), 'Sunday Close'(17:00-22:00), 'Tuesday Close'(18:00-22:00), 'Thursday Close'(18:00-22:00), 'Saturday Close'(18:00-22:00), 'Friday Close'(19:00-22:00). rota_shifts.name is denormalised from the template (supabase/migrations/20260503000001_backfill_shift_names.sql backfills `s.name = t.name`) and rota_published_shifts.name carries it too, so name survives template edits/deletes. CAVEAT: two active bar templates named just 'Monday' and 'Wednesday' cover open-to-close and do NOT contain the word 'Close' — a name ILIKE '%close%' filter would miss those two days entirely. (2) BY TIME: pick max(end_time) per shift_date (accounting for is_overnight), or match end_time against business_hours.closes for that day_of_week — prod business_hours closes at 22:00:00 every day, which matches every active bar closing template's end_time.
why_it_matters: There is NO existing 'closing shift' concept in code — a grep for closing shift / is_closing / closer across src returns nothing relevant. This logic must be written from scratch, and a naive name-match on '%Close%' silently misses Mondays and Wednesdays.
decision_needed: How should 'the closing shift' be defined — by template name, by latest end_time on the date, or by end_time matching business_hours.closes? Recommendation: latest end_time among status='scheduled', is_open_shift=false, department='bar' shifts on that date, because it survives template renames and correctly catches the 'Monday'/'Wednesday' open-to-close templates that name-matching misses.

topic: Rota — departments are no longer constrained to bar/kitchen
detail: The original CHECK `department IN ('bar','kitchen')` was DROPPED by supabase/migrations/20260301120000_rota_shifts_drop_department_check.sql (the `departments` table is now the source of truth). Live prod rota_shift_templates use five departments: bar, kitchen, cleaning, host, runner. rota_shift_templates has its own check dropped by supabase/migrations/20260315000001_rota_shift_templates_drop_department_check.sql.
why_it_matters: Filtering a 'who closed up' query by department must target 'bar' explicitly — assuming only bar/kitchen exist will pull in cleaning/host/runner shifts (e.g. the 'Host' 19:00-22:00 template also ends at 22:00, tying with the bar closer on max(end_time)).

topic: Opening hours — table exists, per day of week
detail: `public.business_hours` — DDL at supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:1889. Columns: id, day_of_week INTEGER (CHECK 0-6, 0=Sunday per src/types/business-hours.ts:11 and DAY_NAMES at :60), opens TIME, closes TIME, kitchen_opens TIME, kitchen_closes TIME, is_closed BOOLEAN. Later additions: is_kitchen_closed BOOLEAN (squashed.sql:10684) and schedule_config JSONB DEFAULT '[]' (squashed.sql:15789). Columns opens/closes/created_at/updated_at were once dropped by a bad cleanup and restored by supabase/migrations/20260104100000_restore_business_hours_columns.sql. Live prod values (verified read-only): open 12:00-22:00 Sat/Sun, 16:00-22:00 Mon-Fri; no day is_closed; Monday has is_kitchen_closed=true. Types: src/types/business-hours.ts:9-21.
why_it_matters: Pub closing time is a single row lookup by day_of_week and is currently 22:00 every day — usable as the reference point for identifying a closing shift.

topic: Opening hours — special/closed days
detail: `public.special_hours` — DDL at supabase/migrations/20251123120000_squashed.sql:3338.
