Columns: id, date DATE, opens, closes, kitchen_opens, kitchen_closes, is_closed BOOLEAN, note TEXT; is_kitchen_closed added at squashed.sql:8186/10627 and schedule_config JSONB at squashed.sql:15793. Type at src/types/business-hours.ts:23-36. Precedence rule is field-by-field COALESCE(special, business) — documented and implemented in `getKitchenWindowForDate` at src/services/business-hours.ts:186-234 (comment at :180-185 notes this mirrors the create_table_booking_v05 RPC). Simpler whole-day override helper: `BusinessHoursService.isSiteOpen(siteId, date)` at src/services/business-hours.ts:758 — checks special_hours first, then business_hours, defaults CLOSED when neither exists. Batched N-date version (3 queries instead of one per date): src/app/actions/missing-cashups.ts:35-73. CRUD: BusinessHoursService.getSpecialHours:575, createSpecialHours:588, updateSpecialHours:667, deleteSpecialHours:729. Also separate from hours: `service_statuses` / service status overrides (business-hours.ts:315-475) for enabling/disabling individual services over a date range.
why_it_matters: Any date-driven feature must honour special_hours (bank holidays, one-off closures) or it will fire on days the pub was shut; reuse getKitchenWindowForDate/isSiteOpen rather than re-deriving precedence.

topic: Opening hours — a DST/UTC trap in the day-of-week derivation
detail: Two different day-of-week derivations coexist. src/services/business-hours.ts:191 uses `new Date(`${date}T00:00:00Z`).getUTCDay()` (timezone-safe). But src/services/business-hours.ts:760 (`isSiteOpen`) uses `new Date(date).getDay()` — server-local — and src/app/actions/missing-cashups.ts:69 uses `date.getDay()` on a date-fns local Date. On a UTC server these agree with London for most of the year but the raw `new Date(date)` parse is exactly the pattern CLAUDE.md forbids.
why_it_matters: New code should copy the getUTCDay() form at business-hours.ts:191, not isSiteOpen's local getDay(), to avoid an off-by-one-day bug that is invisible on a London laptop.

topic: The word "todos" in the requirement has been completely ignored
detail: The owner asked for "checklists AND todos". Every one of the ~70 findings is about recurring, scheduled, compliance-shaped checklist items. Nobody has asked a single question about the todos half: one-off ad-hoc jobs ("chase the Coke delivery", "fix the gents' hand dryer") created by Billy, assigned to a person or left open, with a due date and no recurrence. These have different mechanics — no schedule, no score weight, no window, but they DO need an owner, a due date and a done/dropped state. Trail models this as a separate schedule type (One-Off / Ad Hoc) precisely because it doesn't fit the recurrence engine.
why_it_matters: Half the stated requirement is unspecced. If todos get bolted onto the checklist instance model late, they will either pollute the completion score (a one-off job counted as a missed daily check) or be dropped from v1 and the owner will notice on day one.
decision_needed: Are one-off todos in v1 scope, and do they count towards anyone's completion score or sit outside it entirely?

topic: Nobody asked whether this is a compliance system or an ops tool — and the research has silently assumed compliance
detail: The owner's verbatim requirements mention food safety, EHO, HACCP or SFBB exactly zero times. They asked for checklists, scoring, spot checks and a clock-out email. Yet roughly a third of the research is EHO-defensibility, FHRS ratings, Article 22A-22D, immutable audit trails and 24-month retention. Those requirements are real but they are the researchers' import, not the owner's ask. Compliance-grade (immutable append-only records, inspector view, PDF export of any date range, per-record-type retention, defence capture on a failed temperature) is plausibly 3x the build of an internal ops tool.
why_it_matters: This is the largest uncosted scope decision in the whole exercise, and it is being smuggled in as an assumption rather than surfaced as a choice. If the answer is "internal ops tool", most of the uk-compliance findings become out of scope and the build halves. If the answer is "this replaces our paper SFBB diary", then retention, immutability and export are non-negotiable and the temperature items need typed thresholds and a legal basis per checkpoint.
decision_needed: Is this replacing the pub's paper food-safety records (EHO-facing), or is it an internal ops tool that makes no compliance claim?

topic: The clock-out warning as specified will email the wrong people the wrong list
detail: "On clock-out, warn of incomplete checklist items" is unscoped. A kitchen porter clocking out at 17:00 will, as literally specified, be warned about — and then emailed — the 23:30 close-down checks, the Friday 22:00 check and the toilet check due at 21:00. None of those are theirs and none are late yet. The requirement needs three separate scoping rules that nobody has asked about: (a) which items — only those already past their due time, or everything outstanding today; (b) whose items — assigned to them, done by their shift, or the whole venue; (c) which shift — the pub has day and night shifts and the last person out is the only one who can meaningfully be warned about close-down.
why_it_matters: Get this wrong and every staff member gets a nightly "you missed 9 things" email for jobs that weren't theirs and weren't due. The feature dies of noise in a fortnight and manager@ gets a nightly pile of false accusations. This is the most user-visible part of the requirement and it is the least specified.
decision_needed: At clock-out, do we warn only about items (a) already past due, AND (b) attributable to that person or their shift — with a separate end-of-day sweep for whole-venue misses?

topic: The existing FOH page cannot take a new nav item without breaking FOH-only mode
detail: src/lib/foh/user-mode.ts: isFohOnlyUser() returns true only if EVERY permission the user holds is on module_name === 'table_bookings'. Granting an FOH user a new 'checklists' permission makes that function return false, which turns FOH-only chromeless mode off and drops the full nav and back button onto the bar iPad. Also, "the /FOH page" is itself ambiguous — src/app/(authenticated)/table-bookings/foh/page.tsx is a table-bookings schedule screen gated on table_bookings.view; there is no general FOH home page.
why_it_matters: A concrete, already-present code trap that will be discovered mid-build. It also forces an early decision: either checklists live under the table_bookings permission (wrong, unmaintainable) or isFohOnlyUser() gets rewritten to an allowlist of modules (correct, but touches live chromeless behaviour that FOH staff depend on).
decision_needed: Do we widen isFohOnlyUser() to an allowlist of FOH-permitted modules (table_bookings + checklists + timeclock), accepting a change to live chromeless mode?

topic: manager@the-anchor.pub is hard-coded across the app as an identity, and the requirement asks it to be two contradictory things
detail: manager@the-anchor.pub is hard-coded in at least six places (timeclock.ts MANAGER_IPAD_EMAIL, table-bookings/foh/page.tsx, rota.ts MANAGER_SHIFT_EMAIL, employee-birthdays, feedback route) as both the shared kiosk login AND the manager notification mailbox. The requirement wants the shared login to be the thing all staff use, and simultaneously wants it to receive the cc of everyone's missed-items email. Nobody has asked what ROLE manager@ actually holds. If it holds manager or super_admin, then every staff member standing at the iPad can open the superadmin insights nav item and read everyone's scores — which directly contradicts "keep the record for superadmins".
why_it_matters: The scoring record's confidentiality rests entirely on this. It is also a live security question independent of this project: whatever manager@ can do today, every bar staff member can already do.
decision_needed: What role does manager@the-anchor.pub hold today, and do we need a separate low-privilege shared FOH account so the shared login cannot open the insights/reporting screens?

topic: The PIN we'd attribute scores with can legally be your phone number's last four digits
detail: src/lib/timeclock/pin.ts exports phoneLastFourMatchesPin(pin, ...phones) — the timeclock accepts the last four digits of an employee's stored phone number as their PIN. Colleagues know each other's phone numbers. Every attribution finding in this research assumes a PIN is a person-proof; here it demonstrably is not, and any staff member accused of missed checks can say "someone used my PIN" and be right.
why_it_matters: Attribution is the foundation the entire scoring, spot-check and clock-out-email feature sits on. A shared-secret PIN is fine for clocking in (nobody clocks in for a colleague — it costs them money) but is not fine for a record that leads to a performance conversation. Nobody has surfaced that the existing mechanism is knowingly weak.
decision_needed: For checklist attribution, do we require a properly hashed, self-chosen PIN and drop the phone-last-four fallback — and force a PIN reset for all staff at launch?

topic: employee_id vs user_id — nobody has decided what a completion record points at
detail: AMS has two identity systems: RBAC/auth is keyed on Supabase user_id (users, user_roles, checkUserPermission), while rota, timeclock and payroll are keyed on employee_id. Staff who work behind the bar have employee records but mostly do not have their own Supabase user accounts — they use the shared login. Scores are about employees. Permissions and nav are about users. Not one finding names which key completed_by hangs off, yet every scoring, insight and spot-check query depends on it.
why_it_matters: This is a day-one schema decision that is expensive to reverse and blocks everything downstream. It also determines whether "who's doing them" reporting can join to the rota at all.
decision_needed: Does a checklist completion record store employee_id (joins to rota/timeclock, works with PIN attribution, no Supabase account needed) with user_id only as an optional audit field?

topic: Spot checks: the owner's one novel requirement is the least specified thing in the document
detail: "Items marked spot check applicable; randomise which get spot-checked each day by Billy" has generated one finding (model it as a separate audit entity). Unasked: how many items per day; when is the selection generated and by what seed; does Billy see the selection in advance (if yes, it's not blind; if no, it must appear on his phone); can staff see which items are selected (they must not, or they'll do those three properly); what does a spot check RECORD — pass/fail, a note, a photo; and critically, what happens when a spot check FAILS an item the staff already ticked. Does it retroactively void their completion? Does it hit their score? Does it open a corrective action? Who does it when Billy is on holiday?
why_it_matters: The spot check is the only thing in the whole design that can tell a genuine 100% from a faked 100% — the research's own Ontario finding says self-reported completion validates nothing. It is the single highest-value feature and it currently has no mechanics at all.
decision_needed: For each spot check, define: N items per day, selection visible only to Billy, recorded as pass/fail + note, and — the real question — does a failed spot check void the original tick and dent the completer's score, or only open a corrective action against the venue?

topic: Template versioning and score comparability — absent entirely
detail: Billy will edit checklists constantly: add an item, delete an item, retitle one, change a fridge's acceptable range. No finding addresses what happens to history. If a template is edited in place, last month's completion records point at an item that no longer exists or now means something different, and the 30-day score comparison silently compares different denominators. If items are soft-deleted, an item deleted mid-period must be excluded from the denominator for the days after it went — which is a different rule from Xenia's N/A nullify.
why_it_matters: Trail's whole score is a 30-day rolling average vs the previous 30. That comparison is meaningless the first time Billy adds four items, and nobody will be able to explain why the score dropped. Retrofitting versioning after a year of records is a data migration nobody wants.
decision_needed: Do templates get immutable versions, with each completion record pinned to the template version in force at the time?

topic: There is no inventory of the pub's actual checklists — the discovery has zero real content
detail: Seventy findings on scheduling models, scoring philosophy and EHO law, and not one line about what The Anchor actually checks today. We know four items by accident from the owner's examples (cellar cooler temp, two bottle fridge temps, candles in autumn/winter, toilet checks every 2h, watering plants, 22:00 Fri-Sat check). Nobody has asked Billy for the current paper lists, or how many items there are in total, or how many staff, or how many shifts a day.
why_it_matters: The real project risk is content authoring, not code. Gawande's 5-9 items per pause point is a design constraint you cannot test against without knowing whether Billy's opening list has 6 items or 40. The volume also determines whether the notification/score noise problem is theoretical or fatal. Every scheduling decision above (tolerance fields, meter triggers, closed-day policies) is currently being made against imagined tasks.
decision_needed: Before any schema is designed, will you get Billy to send the actual current checklists — every list, every item, its cadence and who does it?

topic: Trading hours are a hard prerequisite and may not exist as data
detail: "Toilet checks every 2h between open and close" and "22:00 check Fri-Sat only" both require the venue's opening hours as structured, per-weekday, seasonally-variable data. The scheduling research correctly concludes intra-day cadence must be bounded by the day's actual open/close from a trading-hours table — but nobody checked whether AMS has one. If it doesn't, building and maintaining it (including bank holidays, Christmas, early closes, late licences) is a prerequisite project, not a detail.
why_it_matters: It's the load-bearing dependency under two of the eight stated cadences, and it's the kind of thing that gets discovered in week three.
decision_needed: Does AMS hold structured trading hours today — and if not, do we build a trading-hours table first or hard-code the current pattern and accept manual edits?

topic: Day-conditional tasks will be wrong, because the pub's real condition isn't the day of the week
detail: "22:00 check Fri-Sat only" is a proxy for "the nights we're busy and open late". A Thursday private hire, a Christmas party week, a bank holiday Sunday and a quiet washed-out Saturday all break it. AMS already knows about events and private bookings — nobody asked whether a task condition can reference that existing data (e.g. "any night with a private booking or an event") rather than a static BYDAY.
why_it_matters: This is the difference between a rule that stays true and one Billy has to remember to override — and the research is clear that overrides get forgotten and phantom overdues train staff to ignore the list. It's also cheap: we own the events data.
decision_needed: Can task conditions read existing AMS state (event scheduled / private booking / late licence), or are conditions limited to day-of-week and season in v1?

topic: Who defines "AUTUMN/WINTER", and the seasonal toggle nobody will remember to flip
detail: Candles in autumn/winter. Fixed dates (1 Oct–31 Mar) are wrong in a warm October and a dark September. A manager toggle is itself a task that will be forgotten — and a forgotten seasonal toggle means either a phantom daily miss all summer or no candles until someone notices in November. No finding addresses this trade-off, which applies equally to garden-only tasks, fire-lighting checks and heater checks.
why_it_matters: It's the one cadence in the owner's list that has no good automatic answer, and the failure mode (silent no-op or daily false miss) is exactly what kills trust in the list.
decision_needed: Are seasons fixed date ranges with a manual override, and does the system prompt Billy at the boundary to confirm the season has changed?

topic: Staff have one email address on record and it is almost certainly personal
detail: src/types/database.ts has a single email_address on the employee record. The requirement is to email THEM their missed items and cc manager@. That means sending a record of alleged underperformance to a person's personal Gmail, copied to a mailbox the whole team can open (manager@ is the shared login). Unasked: what if the employee has no email; what happens on bounce; is manager@ actually a private mailbox or does everyone have the password; and does an email that says "you missed these" become an employment record the person can subject-access and dispute.
why_it_matters: Emailing performance criticism to a personal address, cc'd to a shared mailbox that colleagues can read, is a real dignity-at-work and data-protection problem — and it's in the requirement verbatim, so it will get built unless it's challenged now.
decision_needed: Is manager@the-anchor.pub a private mailbox or one the team can open — and if the latter, should the cc go to Billy's individual address instead?

topic: Nightly emails to the manager will not be read
detail: cc manager@ on every clock-out means, on a normal night with five staff clocking out, five emails — most listing items that weren't due or weren't theirs (see the clock-out scoping finding). Nobody has proposed the obvious alternative: a single end-of-day digest to Billy at business-day close, listing the venue's genuine misses with reason codes, and individual emails only to the person who actually missed something.
why_it_matters: The manager copy exists so problems get seen. Five emails a night guarantees they don't. This is the cheapest fix in the whole design and it hasn't been raised.
decision_needed: Do we replace the per-clock-out cc with one end-of-day digest to Billy, keeping the individual email to the staff member?

topic: The value-input items name physical assets, implying an equipment register nobody has scoped
detail: "Cellar cooler temp, two bottle fridge temps" are three named pieces of equipment, each with its own acceptable range, its own failure meaning, and its own lifecycle. The compliance research is emphatic that the cellar cooler is a QUALITY checkpoint at 11–13°C, not the 8°C food-safety limit — apply the wrong band and it fails every single day and staff learn to ignore red. Unasked: what happens when a fridge is broken, emptied, switched off overnight, or replaced; is there a "not in use" state that suppresses the check without scoring a miss; who sets the ranges; and does "bottle fridge 1 / bottle fridge 2" survive someone swapping them round.
why_it_matters: Three assets today is fine hard-coded; the moment there's a fourth fridge or a broken one, a hard-coded list produces daily false failures — the exact alert fatigue the research warns kills these systems.
decision_needed: Do we build a small equipment register (name, type, acceptable range, in-service flag) with checks pointing at it, rather than putting ranges on the task template?

topic: "Surface problems... and with whom" means naming staff on a screen the whole team uses
detail: The requirement says the checklists UI should show what needs addressing and with whom. The FOH device is the shared iPad on the bar. Rendering "Outstanding: toilet check 21:00 — Sam" on that screen publishes one person's failings to every colleague and any customer who can see the bar. The research flagged that Trail deliberately hides scores from staff and that a leaderboard reads as public shaming, but nobody connected that to this specific requirement or to the fact that the surface in question is a shared screen.
why_it_matters: This is the requirement most likely to make staff hate the system on day one, and it's a one-line design decision to avoid.
decision_needed: Does the shared FOH screen show only what's outstanding (no names), with the "with whom" visible only in the superadmin insights view?

topic: Nobody has asked whether the staff will be told they're being scored
detail: The owner wants to score people on completion and keep the record for superadmins. That is worker monitoring: a named, retained, per-person performance record generated by software. UK GDPR requires a lawful basis identified up front, transparency, and — per the ICO's own worked example — that you do not judge performance solely on what the system can see while ignoring the work done outside it. None of the process questions have been asked: is there a privacy notice; will staff be told at launch or will they find out from an email; can a staff member see their own record; how long is it kept; what happens to it when they leave.
why_it_matters: Discovering the score's existence via an automated "you missed these" email to your personal address is the worst possible launch. The legal exposure is modest for a single pub, but the trust damage is not, and the fix (tell people, one page, before launch) is free.
decision_needed: Will staff be told in writing before launch that checklist completion is recorded and scored per person, and can they see their own record?

topic: Retention of the personal record is undefined — "keep the record for superadmins" has no end date
detail: The compliance research settled on 24 months for food-safety records, but that's the CHECK record, not the PERSON score. Nobody has asked how long a named individual's completion history is retained, whether it survives their leaving, or whether photos (which will capture staff and customers) have a shorter clock. These are three different retention periods on the same table.
why_it_matters: "Forever" is the default if nobody decides, and a leaver's performance record sitting in Supabase indefinitely is both a subject-access liability and pointless.
decision_needed: Set retention separately for: the check record (24 months), the personal score attribution (12 months / deleted on leaving + 6 months), and photo evidence (30-90 days) — agreed?

topic: No success criteria, no adoption plan, no rollback
detail: Nothing in the discovery says how we'd know in three months whether this worked, or what happens if staff don't use it. The Ontario finding says a fully-adopted checklist can change nothing while reporting near-100% compliance — so "completion rate is high" is explicitly not evidence of success. There's no proposal to run paper in parallel during bedding-in, no pilot scope (one list first vs all of them), and no answer to "what do we turn off if it's ignored".
why_it_matters: This determines the shape of v1 more than any scheduling model does. A pilot of one list (opening checks) for two weeks tests attribution, the clock-out email, the score and the spot check end-to-end for a fraction of the build — and the content authoring can then follow the evidence.
decision_needed: Do we pilot with a single list (opening checks) for two weeks before building the insights and scoring layer, and what number tells us it worked?
